preview: Text 1 Researcher Mei Tanaka examined hybrid work policies in companies across Japan. She argues that allowing employees to work some days…
searchText: based on the texts, how would park and alvarez (text 2) most likely respond to the claim made in text 1? text 1 researcher mei tanaka examined hybrid work policies in companies across japan. she argues that allowing employees to work some days from home leads to a noticeable improvement in productivity, since workers can focus during quiet hours and avoid stressful commutes. therefore, companies that permit hybrid schedules tend to outperform those that require workers to be in the office every day. text 2 workplaces are more efficient when teams can communicate face-to-face. however, allowing employees to work from home some days may have hidden costs. a 2023 study by economists lin park and diego alvarez found that, when employees work remotely, they often miss informal hallway conversations that help solve problems quickly. as a result, productivity gains measured in isolation may not capture the full picture of a team's performance.

preview: Text 1 Although nature writing is one of the most popular genres in the United States, academic critics have long treated it as marginal. W…
searchText: based on the two texts, how would the author of text 1 most likely regard the situation presented in the underlined sentence in text 2? text 1 although nature writing is one of the most popular genres in the united states, academic critics have long treated it as marginal. within nature writing, field guides receive the least scholarly respect of all, no matter how carefully they are written. this is especially true of guides devoted to backyard wildlife, whose accessibility and ecological detail are often dismissed as merely practical. text 2 in her 1962 book watching songbirds , naturalist helen carver transformed a regional field guide into something more. her crisp, observational prose lifts the text beyond a mere catalog of species. yet despite its undeniable craft, carver's book has received virtually no attention from literary critics.

preview: Text 1 In a study of bird intelligence, researcher Alex Taylor and colleagues observed New Caledonian crows bending wire into hooks to retr…
searchText: based on the texts, how would the author of text 2 most likely respond to the underlined portion of text 1? text 1 in a study of bird intelligence, researcher alex taylor and colleagues observed new caledonian crows bending wire into hooks to retrieve food from narrow tubes. the crows had not been shown how to do this, and they often invented new tool shapes when an existing tool didn’t work. the crows appeared to be genuinely creative — inventing solutions in much the way that human problem-solvers do . text 2 crows do not have a neocortex of the kind associated with human creative thought. still, taylor and his team have shown that crows can flexibly modify tools to fit new problems. if we are willing to call this kind of behavior “creative,” we should also be careful: the cognitive processes that underlie it are almost certainly far simpler than those involved in human invention.

preview: Text 1 Scholars studying the films of Brazilian director Glauber Rocha have grappled with the sprawling nature of his work, which spans mor…
searchText: which choice best describes a difference in how the author of text 1 and the author of text 2 view the study of rocha's films? text 1 scholars studying the films of brazilian director glauber rocha have grappled with the sprawling nature of his work, which spans more than a dozen feature films. it is most useful, however, to consider rocha's career as a director in three distinct periods, each with its own thematic and stylistic identity: his early experimental work in the 1960s, the politically driven films of the 1970s, and the more reflective late films from the 1980s onward. text 2 it is tempting to organize the body of glauber rocha's films into tidy stages. however, critics who sort rocha's films into clear-cut phases miss possible threads that run through his entire career. such an approach can also flatten meaningful contrasts between films made close together in time.

preview: Text 1 In the winter of 1888, a young schoolteacher named Minnie Freeman led her students through a fierce blizzard to safety on the Nebras…
searchText: based on the texts, both authors would most likely agree with which statement? text 1 in the winter of 1888, a young schoolteacher named minnie freeman led her students through a fierce blizzard to safety on the nebraska prairie. although she was only nineteen at the time, her quick thinking made her a celebrated figure across the american midwest. in the decades that followed, freeman's story was retold in songs, poems, and a memorial statue placed near the schoolhouse where she taught. text 2 historian carmen diaz set out to verify the details of minnie freeman's famous walk and discovered that contemporary sources offered very different versions of events. many later accounts seemed to add dramatic flourishes that older newspapers did not include. while freeman remains a beloved figure in nebraska memory, diaz cautions that some of the most familiar details about her may be later inventions.

preview: Text 1 Many fish species, such as carp, can thrive in a wide range of waterways. By contrast, certain stream-dwelling salamander species ar…
searchText: based on the texts, both authors would most likely agree with which statement? text 1 many fish species, such as carp, can thrive in a wide range of waterways. by contrast, certain stream-dwelling salamander species are highly restricted in where they can live. this is because many of these salamanders rely on cold, oxygen-rich water found only in mountain streams. therefore, these salamanders struggle to survive in lowland streams where the water is warmer. text 2 a recent investigation analyzed observations of more than 200 stream salamander species to understand why they live in such narrow ranges. researchers noticed that when several salamander species coexisted in a watershed, each tended to occupy a smaller area than it would alone. the team concluded that competition among species, rather than water temperature, often limits where these salamanders can live.

preview: Text 1 Dutch painters of the early 1600s seldom included tools of their craft in self-portraits. Painting had not yet attained high social…
searchText: based on the texts, how would the author of text 2 most likely respond to the underlined claim in text 1? text 1 dutch painters of the early 1600s seldom included tools of their craft in self-portraits. painting had not yet attained high social status, so most painters chose to emphasize their education or fine clothing instead. in the city of haarlem, the first painter to depict himself with a palette was frans hals. a portrait of his from about 1640 shows hals holding a brush in mid-stroke. text 2 among haarlem painters, references to the act of painting were uncommon in self-portraits until the mid-seventeenth century. however, judith leyster's self-portrait , completed around 1633, stands out as an earlier example. in the painting, leyster turns toward the viewer with a brush in one hand and a palette in the other, while a half-finished canvas rests on her easel.

preview: Text 1 Researchers studying ancient Egyptian wall paintings have looked to scenes of daily life to understand the lives of farmers and craf…
searchText: based on the texts, what would sara ahmed (text 2) most likely say about the interpretation presented in the underlined portion of text 1? text 1 researchers studying ancient egyptian wall paintings have looked to scenes of daily life to understand the lives of farmers and craftspeople. these richly painted murals show people, animals, and crops in remarkable detail— scholars have interpreted differences in clothing as evidence of social class . some historians have even used these murals to identify potential occupations and family roles in ancient households. text 2 egyptologist sara ahmed has argued that reading egyptian wall paintings as faithful records of social class can overlook the choices of the artists who made them. the paintings, ahmed suggests, follow visual conventions that exaggerate or stylize details, and many depictions of clothing reflect artistic tradition rather than what people actually wore.

preview: Text 1 A team led by economist Maya Lindgren reports that adopting a four-day workweek could deliver significant gains for businesses and w…
searchText: which choice best describes a difference in how the author of text 1 and the author of text 2 view lindgren's team's study? text 1 a team led by economist maya lindgren reports that adopting a four-day workweek could deliver significant gains for businesses and workers alike. according to their study, companies that shortened their schedules saw a 35% drop in employee burnout and only a small dip in productivity—results the authors call "a clear victory for both well-being and the bottom line." text 2 while lindgren's team's results are encouraging, it's worth considering the impact on customer service. the shortened schedule means fewer hours available for client interaction, and some businesses may struggle to cover demand. any policy promoting a four-day workweek should therefore also address how companies can maintain reliable service to the people who depend on them.

preview: Text 1 Mexican muralist Diego Rivera's love of pre-Columbian art is most clearly visible in the imagery and symbols he wove into his murals…
searchText: based on the texts, both authors would most likely agree with which statement? text 1 mexican muralist diego rivera's love of pre-columbian art is most clearly visible in the imagery and symbols he wove into his murals. rivera collected aztec and mayan artifacts throughout his life, drawing inspiration from sculptures, codices, and stonework. with his choice of motifs, rivera helped bring ancient mexican traditions to modern audiences. text 2 pre-columbian influence runs through all of rivera's work, but it is most visible in the bold compositional style he used. rivera often arranged figures in flat, layered planes that recall the panels of mesoamerican codices. when painting public walls, rivera rarely centered a single dominant figure. instead, he organized scenes in stacked registers, the way ancient artisans did.

preview: Text 1 For years, French chefs have debated whether classic cuisine should accept influences from other cooking traditions, such as Japanes…
searchText: based on the texts, how would the author of text 2 most likely regard the perspective of the french traditionalists, as described in text 1? text 1 for years, french chefs have debated whether classic cuisine should accept influences from other cooking traditions, such as japanese cooking. many "traditionalists" hold that french cuisine is defined by the techniques codified by auguste escoffier in the early 1900s. they regard escoffier's recipes, which set the standard for french restaurants, as a benchmark that today's chefs should still honor. text 2 french cuisine is not simply a continuation of older provincial cooking. in fact, escoffier's signature dishes themselves drew on imported ingredients and techniques—some from north africa, others from asia—that had only recently reached france. because french cuisine has always been a blended tradition, today's chefs should not be barred from drawing on japanese or any other influences.

preview: Text 1 American photographer Gordon Parks is best known for his powerful portraits of public figures, such as his iconic images of Muhammad…
searchText: based on the texts, both authors would most likely agree with which statement? text 1 american photographer gordon parks is best known for his powerful portraits of public figures, such as his iconic images of muhammad ali and malcolm x. although parks photographed many other subjects, his career as a photographer is best represented by the works in which he captured well-known historical figures. text 2 critics have often overlooked the many fashion photographs gordon parks made for magazines like vogue . parks likely took these fashion images regularly during his long career. he is celebrated for his portraits, but parks likely supported himself financially through commercial fashion work for editors who paid him to shoot models and clothing. thus, his fashion photographs are a central aspect of his career.

preview: Text 1 Today the tomato is central to Italian cooking, but its rise to popularity was gradual. Spanish traders brought tomatoes from Mexico…
searchText: based on the texts, the author of text 1 and the author of text 2 would most likely agree with which statement? text 1 today the tomato is central to italian cooking, but its rise to popularity was gradual. spanish traders brought tomatoes from mexico to southern italy in the 1500s. at that time, however, people in many regions had little contact with the southern coast, and traditional cooks distrusted unfamiliar plants. thus, the tomato remained little used by most italians until the late 1700s. text 2 the tomato's slow adoption in italian kitchens is mainly due to the nature of the plant itself. the tomato belongs to the same family as several poisonous nightshades, and many europeans assumed it must also be dangerous. knowledge that ripe tomatoes were safe to eat needed to spread before the fruit could grow in popularity. the publication of widely shared cookbooks in the late 1700s directly fueled this shift.

preview: Text 1 Most geologists agree that Saturn's rings were formed after the breakup of one of Saturn's moons. This event likely scattered ice an…
searchText: which choice best describes a difference in how the author of text 1 and the author of text 2 view the evidence for the formation of saturn's rings? text 1 most geologists agree that saturn's rings were formed after the breakup of one of saturn's moons. this event likely scattered ice and rock into orbit around the planet. based on widely accepted models of ring formation, scientists believe the rings settled into their current shape over the course of hundreds of millions of years. text 2 researchers at the university of colorado recently used a new simulation to model how saturn's rings could have formed. although earlier simulations exist, the colorado team's runs were far more detailed. they found that the rings were probably not the product of a slow process. instead, it is likely that the ring system took shape within only a few decades after the moon's destruction.

preview: Text 1 Imagine you and a coworker are trying to choose a meeting time for a project. When people make joint scheduling decisions like this,…
searchText: based on the texts, what response would the author of text 2 most likely suggest for someone in the situation described in the underlined sentence in text 1? text 1 imagine you and a coworker are trying to choose a meeting time for a project. when people make joint scheduling decisions like this, they often don't share their real availability. instead, they say any time would work because they think this response makes them seem more accommodating and easier to work with. text 2 research shows that people who don't share their actual preferences when scheduling with others aren't viewed as more cooperative. in fact, saying you have no preference makes scheduling harder for the other person. it can also lead them to feel less satisfied with the final plan—and with you.

preview: Text 1 Goldfinches are famous for their bright yellow plumage, but they are actually born with dull olive-gray feathers. Their yellow color…
searchText: based on the texts, how would the ornithologist in text 2 most likely respond to the author's conclusion in text 1? text 1 goldfinches are famous for their bright yellow plumage, but they are actually born with dull olive-gray feathers. their yellow color comes from eating seeds, but the seeds themselves aren't naturally yellow. animals can't produce carotenoids, the pigments responsible for the yellow hue. the plants that produce the seeds, however, can make these pigments. thus, the more yellow a goldfinch, the more seeds it has eaten. text 2 ornithologist priya desai has found that goldfinches actively redistribute pigments to make themselves look more yellow. a gland near the base of their wing stores pigments drawn from their diet. when the birds preen, they spread these pigments through their feathers. goldfinches may do this most during the breeding season, when looking yellower offers an advantage.

preview: Text 1 Philosopher Thomas Reid argued that human beings possess what he termed “first principles”—foundational beliefs, such as the reliabi…
searchText: based on the texts, how would the author of text 1 most likely respond to proponents of the philosophical stance outlined in text 2? text 1 philosopher thomas reid argued that human beings possess what he termed “first principles”—foundational beliefs, such as the reliability of memory and the existence of the physical world, that all people necessarily accept in practice even when they claim to doubt them. according to reid, these convictions form the bedrock of all reasoning, and any philosophical system that attempts to deny them undermines itself, since the denial must rely on the very faculties it rejects. text 2 radical skepticism holds that genuine knowledge of an external reality is impossible because every belief we hold could, in principle, be the product of deception or error. proponents acknowledge that abandoning ordinary convictions feels strange, but they insist that no argument has ever been advanced to show why those convictions deserve to be trusted simply because we cannot help relying on them in daily life.

preview: Text 1 Video games have grown into a multibillion-dollar industry, but they shouldn’t be considered a form of storytelling. Storytelling, b…
searchText: based on the texts, how would the author of text 2 most likely respond to the overall argument presented in text 1? text 1 video games have grown into a multibillion-dollar industry, but they shouldn’t be considered a form of storytelling. storytelling, by definition, conveys a fixed narrative through which a creator’s vision is communicated to an audience; in video games, however, the player’s choices determine what happens, so any resulting “story” is largely accidental. games are better understood as toys: structured systems built to be played with rather than narratives meant to be received. text 2 the interactive elements of video games are precisely what allow them to tell stories impossible in other media. a designer arranges environments, characters, and consequences so that the player’s decisions illuminate themes the designer has carefully prepared in advance. games such as disco elysium and what remains of edith finch deliver narratives whose meaning depends on the player’s participation, not on the absence of authorial intent.

preview: Text 1 Because the system of knotted cords known as khipu fell out of use after the Spanish conquest of the Inca Empire, scholars have long…
searchText: based on the texts, how would the author of text 2 most likely characterize hyland’s proposal, as described in text 1? text 1 because the system of knotted cords known as khipu fell out of use after the spanish conquest of the inca empire, scholars have long debated how meaning was encoded in its colored strings and knots. many researchers had assumed that khipu recorded only numerical information—tallies of tribute, population, and goods. in 2005, anthropologist sabine hyland proposed instead that certain khipu functioned as a phonetic writing system, with each cord representing a distinct syllable. text 2 recent comparative analyses of surviving khipu point to a more flexible picture. depending on context, a single cord could carry numerical, narrative, or even logographic content: the same configuration might mark a quantity of maize in one inventory and stand for a place-name in another. for an inca reader, interpreting a khipu meant determining at each cord which of several functions was being invoked.

preview: Text 1 Many studies in behavioral economics have shown that people will pay to reduce uncertainty about an outcome even when the additional…
searchText: based on the texts, how would acuña and colleagues (text 2) most likely respond to the prevailing view discussed in text 1? text 1 many studies in behavioral economics have shown that people will pay to reduce uncertainty about an outcome even when the additional information does not change the decisions available to them. such findings have supported the prevailing view among researchers: that the aversion to uncertainty represents a fundamental preference for resolution itself, independent of any practical use to which the resolved information might be put. text 2 while accepting that uncertainty is uncomfortable, mariana acuña and colleagues conducted an experiment to test whether describing outcomes as relevant to future planning would alter participants’ willingness to pay for early information. they found that when an outcome was framed as bearing on a forthcoming decision, participants paid noticeably more to learn it in advance than when the same outcome was framed as unrelated to anything they would later choose.

preview: Text 1 Tardigrades are microscopic invertebrates known for surviving in punishing environments—from boiling springs to the vacuum of space.…
searchText: based on the texts, lindholm’s team and the author of text 2 would most likely agree with which statement about tardigrades? text 1 tardigrades are microscopic invertebrates known for surviving in punishing environments—from boiling springs to the vacuum of space. so iris lindholm and her team were puzzled when sediment samples retrieved from an unusually acidic hydrothermal vent in the atlantic appeared to contain no tardigrades. the finding does not prove that no tardigrades inhabit such vents, but lindholm’s team argues that it does indicate that these particular conditions impose severe limits on tardigrade survival. text 2 tardigrades have been recovered from nearly every habitat ever sampled, so it is implausible that they would be entirely absent from lindholm’s vent site, however unusual its chemistry. most likely there were so few individuals in the sediment that available techniques could not register them. even so, because ordinary marine sediments routinely contain hundreds of tardigrades per gram, finding so few at the vent would still demonstrate how forbidding its conditions are.

preview: Text 1 The independent bookstore sector is expanding in both store count and event programming. With consumers facing endless online option…
searchText: based on the texts, both authors would most likely agree with which statement? text 1 the independent bookstore sector is expanding in both store count and event programming. with consumers facing endless online options, owners are working to make in-store visits a memorable communal experience worth recounting. this trend is connected to the wider “experiential economy,” in which many people prefer to spend money on lived activities. to remain profitable, bookstores increasingly design their spaces around reader events and curated atmosphere, not just inventory. text 2 author appearances are becoming a more significant part of writers’ livelihoods. one driver of this shift is the spread of e-book subscription services, which give readers access to vast libraries for a fixed monthly fee and reduce sales of individual print copies. with this change in reading habits, many authors now depend heavily on income from in-person events such as readings and signings.

preview: Text 1 A series of excavations at the Bluefish Caves in northern Yukon has challenged the long-standing belief that humans first entered th…
searchText: based on the texts, how would the author of text 2 most likely respond to the underlined claim in text 1? text 1 a series of excavations at the bluefish caves in northern yukon has challenged the long-standing belief that humans first entered the americas roughly 15,000 years ago. dozens of mammoth and horse bones recovered from the cave floors bear thin parallel grooves dated to as much as 24,000 years ago, revealing that humans were already butchering large game in the region millennia before the conventionally accepted arrival date. text 2 the bluefish caves bones are striking, but it may be premature to read them as proof of human butchery. similar grooves can be produced by carnivores gnawing through dried tendon or by stones rolling across bone during freeze-thaw cycles. moreover, the cave deposits have so far yielded no stone tools, hearth remains, or other independent traces of human occupation from the relevant period.

preview: Text 1 The everyday sense that information cannot travel faster than light is one of the firmest commitments of modern physics, yet it sits…
searchText: based on the texts, what would the author of text 1 most likely say about the experiment described in text 2? text 1 the everyday sense that information cannot travel faster than light is one of the firmest commitments of modern physics, yet it sits uneasily with quantum mechanics. pairs of particles can be prepared so that measuring one instantly determines the state of the other, regardless of how far apart they are. most physicists believe that this correlation cannot be used to send a signal, but exactly which feature of nature prevents it remains, in the standard theory, a matter of interpretation rather than derivation. text 2 in 2022, researchers led by hiroto kawaguchi devised a tabletop test of whether subtle modifications of standard quantum measurements could be used to transmit information between separated detectors. after thousands of trials with entangled photon pairs, no transmission was observed: the correlations behaved exactly as standard theory predicts. as kawaguchi noted, however, “the result confirms the limit without telling us where the limit comes from.”

preview: Text 1 One difficulty faced by researchers comparing rates of homelessness across countries is that nations may define homelessness differe…
searchText: based on the texts, how would the author of text 2 most likely respond to the problem presented in text 1? text 1 one difficulty faced by researchers comparing rates of homelessness across countries is that nations may define homelessness differently. some governments count only individuals sleeping in streets or shelters; others include people living temporarily with friends, in cars, or in informal settlements. sweden, for example, uses four broad categories spanning short-term and long-term housing instability, while several other countries register only those who appear on shelter rolls. this variation makes cross-national comparisons of homelessness particularly difficult. text 2 recently, a working group convened by several international housing agencies issued a unified framework for measuring homelessness across countries. the framework, known as the “global homelessness typology,” specifies common categories—rough sleeping, emergency accommodation, and severely insecure housing—and provides explicit criteria for placing individuals within them, enabling researchers to make more meaningful international comparisons.

preview: Text 1 Electronic dance music, in which producers assemble tracks using software and audio samples, isn’t really music in any serious sense…
searchText: based on the texts, how would the author of text 2 most likely respond to the claims of the author of text 1? text 1 electronic dance music, in which producers assemble tracks using software and audio samples, isn’t really music in any serious sense. it doesn’t demand the kind of skill that performing on a traditional instrument requires. “composing” on a laptop with a sequencer and a library of loops is far easier than mastering a violin: the software is doing the difficult work. text 2 the programs used to create electronic dance music involve much more than dragging a few loops together. beyond knowing the foundations of harmony, rhythm, and arrangement, producers must master complex digital audio workstations, synthesis techniques, and sound design. many producers begin a track by writing melodies and chord progressions on paper or at a keyboard before shaping them on the computer, where they can apply textures and effects that would otherwise require an entire studio of instruments.

preview: Text 1 Relatively little is known about when Mars lost most of its early atmosphere—a transition that turned a once temperate planet into t…
searchText: based on the texts, how would the author of text 2 most likely respond to what “some scientists maintain” as described in text 1? text 1 relatively little is known about when mars lost most of its early atmosphere—a transition that turned a once temperate planet into the cold, thin-aired world observed today. some scientists maintain that the bulk of this loss occurred about 4 billion years ago, often citing simulations of martian magnetic field decay that predict rapid stripping of atmospheric gases by the solar wind once the planet’s magnetic dynamo shut down. text 2 ultimately, any plausible account of when mars lost its atmosphere must rest on direct chemical evidence from the planet itself. researcher diego hurtado and his team measured ratios of light to heavy isotopes of argon trapped in ancient martian meteorites and, based on the data, compellingly argue that substantial atmospheric loss was already well underway approximately 4.4 billion years ago—considerably earlier than the dynamo-decay scenario allows.

preview: Text 1 Japanese woodblock printmaker Katsushika Hokusai insisted that the proofs of his designs be inspected against his own carefully chos…
searchText: based on the texts, both authors would most likely agree with which statement? text 1 japanese woodblock printmaker katsushika hokusai insisted that the proofs of his designs be inspected against his own carefully chosen pigment mixtures before any commercial edition was issued. like many ukiyo-e artists of his generation, hokusai used custom-blended hues that stood apart from the brighter, formulaic palettes favored by the major publishing houses. these artists likely embraced their distinctive color schemes in order to set their works apart from what mass-market production at the time treated as standard. text 2 ukiyo-e printmakers often concentrated on the interplay of line and atmosphere in their compositions. those who selected custom-blended pigments instead of the more conventional publisher palettes likely did so for aesthetic reasons: a particular shade was chosen because it would harmonize with the mood or subject of a given print. stock pigment mixtures, by contrast, could overwhelm the subtleties an artist had carefully built into a design.

preview: Text 1 In a study of the effects of solitude on well-being, Camille Renard reported that participants’ sense of contentment tended to rise…
searchText: based on the texts, how would hammond (text 2) most likely respond to the conclusion renard (text 1) reached after her further analysis? text 1 in a study of the effects of solitude on well-being, camille renard reported that participants’ sense of contentment tended to rise when they spent up to one hour alone each day and began to fall when daily solitude exceeded three hours. after further analysis, renard concluded that this drop in contentment occurred mainly when individuals filled their solitary time with activities she defined as passive, such as scrolling through social media or watching streaming videos. text 2 psychologist idris hammond warns against prescribing an ideal amount of time alone. the human need for both companionship and quiet reflection is universal, but hammond stresses that individuals differ in what they find restorative. furthermore, he points out that there is no objective definition of what counts as a passive activity; scrolling through social media might function as restorative connection for some people and as draining distraction for others.

preview: Text 1 In 2019, a team led by Hana Park hypothesized that the iron meteorites known as IIE chondrites originated from a single, partially d…
searchText: based on the texts, how would avram likely respond to park’s hypothesis, as presented in text 1? text 1 in 2019, a team led by hana park hypothesized that the iron meteorites known as iie chondrites originated from a single, partially differentiated parent body in the early solar system. as evidence, park cited the strikingly similar oxygen isotope ratios found in iie samples and in surface samples returned in 2010 from the asteroid itokawa, whose interior is believed to have undergone only modest internal heating. text 2 although the oxygen isotope ratios of iie chondrites do resemble those of itokawa, several other small asteroids show comparable values. moreover, some iie samples contain large inclusions of pyroxene crystals, as lior avram points out, whereas a partially differentiated parent body would be expected to lack such inclusions. given the prevalence of these pyroxene textures across separate iie specimens, avram argues that two distinct parent bodies are a more plausible explanation.

preview: Text 1 When ride-hailing services first entered urban transportation markets, their operators frequently claimed that the services would re…
searchText: based on the texts, how would ostberg and marchetti (text 2) most likely respond to anand’s findings (text 1)? text 1 when ride-hailing services first entered urban transportation markets, their operators frequently claimed that the services would reduce overall congestion by encouraging passengers to give up private cars. economist vivek anand examined this notion in the context of the atlanta metropolitan area. he estimated a hypothetical city in which ride-hailing achieved its highest plausible market share and found that average downtown travel times would lengthen during peak hours. text 2 transportation economists linnea ostberg and hugo marchetti have argued that research on the effects of ride-hailing has focused excessively on short-run congestion effects, which tend to be unfavorable to commuters. drawing on data from stockholm over a span of nearly a decade, they show that, in the long term, sustained ride-hailing availability is associated with reduced household car ownership and modest improvements in peak-hour traffic flow.

preview: Text 1 Choreographer Loïe Fuller was perhaps the first stage artist to argue that costume fabric should be treated as a sculptural medium,…
searchText: based on the texts, how would the author of text 2 most likely respond to the claim about fuller’s level of technical expertise made by bremner in text 1? text 1 choreographer loïe fuller was perhaps the first stage artist to argue that costume fabric should be treated as a sculptural medium, shaped in motion alongside light and music to produce a unified theatrical effect. researcher iris bremner, however, has noted that fuller lacked technical expertise in fabric manipulation ; as a result, bremner argues, fuller’s ideas called for stage techniques that did not become workable until the introduction of new lightweight silks in the 1890s. text 2 loïe fuller was not a novice in the practice of fabric handling. on the contrary, it was precisely her early exposure to costume work that nourished her theory of fabric as a sculptural element. while employed as an assistant in a small touring company in her youth, fuller spent long hours rigging, tinting, and rehearsing with bolts of stage silk. this sustained, hands-on experience developed her sense of what was possible in the coordination of fabric, light, and motion onstage.

preview: Text 1 Monarch butterflies in eastern North America rely on stands of milkweed as the sole host plant for their caterpillars, but expanding…
searchText: based on the texts, how would the researchers in text 2 most likely respond to the conclusion presented in the underlined portion of text 1? text 1 monarch butterflies in eastern north america rely on stands of milkweed as the sole host plant for their caterpillars, but expanding row-crop agriculture has eliminated much of this milkweed across the species’ breeding range, raising concerns about the future of the migration. a 2021 review of population counts at mexican overwintering sites concluded that monarch populations dependent on the historic breeding range are at substantial risk of collapse within the next several decades . text 2 long-term monitoring by researchers from the ontario lepidopterists’ association indicates that monarchs along the northern fringe of the breeding range have remained surprisingly stable in recent years despite continued losses of agricultural milkweed. the researchers attribute this stability in part to a behavioral shift: in addition to laying eggs on common milkweed, females in this region have begun depositing significant numbers of eggs on swamp milkweed and on butterfly weed growing along roadsides and ditches.

preview: Text 1 A small, unusual fossil preserved in 110-million-year-old lake sediments belongs to the extinct species Cretolepia volans . The C. v…
searchText: based on the texts, what would the paleontologists in text 2 most likely say about the researchers’ initial proposal in text 1? text 1 a small, unusual fossil preserved in 110-million-year-old lake sediments belongs to the extinct species cretolepia volans . the c. volans fossil consists of a delicate ribcage flanked by two narrow, blade-shaped projections that extend outward from the sides of the trunk. because these projections resemble the wing membranes of certain modern reptiles, researchers initially proposed that the animal might be the earliest known gliding lizard. text 2 paleontologists were intrigued to recover a second small fossil similar to the strange c. volans specimen, this one preserving impressions of the tissue surrounding the blade-shaped projections. detailed examination of both fossils revealed soft-tissue features that are characteristic of certain small fish rather than gliding reptiles. therefore, paleontologists now think that the two specimens were probably unusual fish whose stiffened side fins happened to give their bodies a winglike outline.

preview: Text 1 In 1961 director Franco Zeffirelli mounted an influential production of Verdi’s opera Aida that has since served as a template for h…
searchText: based on the texts, how would the author of text 2 most likely respond to the underlined claim in text 1? text 1 in 1961 director franco zeffirelli mounted an influential production of verdi’s opera aida that has since served as a template for hundreds of opera houses around the world, which continue to stage aida in lavish nineteenth-century style each season. but the work is mired in an outdated and stereotyped image of ancient egypt, so it should no longer be performed. opera needs to invent new traditions if it hopes to remain meaningful to contemporary audiences. text 2 aida is indeed dated, but it should be retained because it remains a perennial favorite and provides essential income for many opera companies. although the work’s imagery can feel anachronistic, there are inventive ways to reframe it. for example, conductor soraya mukami successfully reimagined the opera in a contemporary setting. her staging, aida reimagined , places the story in a modern museum and has gained steady acclaim since opening in 2018.

preview: Text 1 It seems clear that adults can become fluent in a new language only through constant immersive interaction with native speakers. Stu…
searchText: based on the texts, what would the researchers in text 2 most likely say about the claim underlined in text 1? text 1 it seems clear that adults can become fluent in a new language only through constant immersive interaction with native speakers. studies of immigrants, for example, repeatedly show that those who live and work daily among speakers of the local language tend to acquire it more quickly than those who do not. likewise, students who participate in study-abroad programs are found to make faster gains in conversational fluency than students who do not. text 2 in a long-term study, researchers mei park and daniel ortega tracked adult learners who used a spaced-repetition app for forty minutes a day but had no in-person contact with native speakers. after eighteen months, more than half of the learners reached conversational fluency in the target language, as measured by independent oral assessments. the researchers concluded that immersive interaction is not required for adult learners to reach high levels of fluency.

preview: Text 1 The increasing reliance on engineered microbial consortia—assemblages of multiple bacterial strains designed to perform tasks such a…
searchText: based on the texts, how would the author of text 2 most likely respond to the assertion in the underlined portion of text 1? text 1 the increasing reliance on engineered microbial consortia—assemblages of multiple bacterial strains designed to perform tasks such as breaking down agricultural waste—has outpaced careful study of their ecological consequences. predictions based on the individual strains involved are inadequate: combining strains can shift their metabolic outputs so dramatically that harmless bacteria together produce a consortium that is anything but . text 2 the risk of amplified toxicity in engineered consortia compared with that of their component strains has rightly prompted scrutiny, but the outcomes of strain combination vary by case. for example, a consortium pairing bacillus subtilis with a soil pseudomonas strain retained the nitrogen-fixing activity prized in agriculture while suppressing a toxin that pseudomonas normally secretes on its own.

preview: Text 1 For all its vivid storytelling, William L. Shirer's 1960 history The Rise and Fall of the Third Reich exhibits notable shortcomings…
searchText: which choice best describes a difference in how the authors of text 1 and text 2 view william l. shirer's the rise and fall of the third reich ? text 1 for all its vivid storytelling, william l. shirer's 1960 history the rise and fall of the third reich exhibits notable shortcomings as a scholarly work. by drawing almost exclusively on captured german records and the memoirs of nazi officials, shirer gives readers little sense of how political life in weimar germany shaped the events that followed. had shirer engaged with the social-history scholarship that was already emerging in his time, his account would not have been so narrowly focused on the actions of a handful of leaders. text 2 william l. shirer's the rise and fall of the third reich is a vivid if old-fashioned introduction to nazi germany. shirer's command of his archival sources is impressive, but his underlying thesis—that the third reich was the inevitable outgrowth of deep flaws in the german national character—is unconvincingly sweeping.

preview: Text 1 Elena Marquez and colleagues observed seven captive bottlenose dolphins ( Tursiops truncatus ) outfitted with experimental suction-c…
searchText: based on the texts, how would the author of text 2 most likely respond to the underlined claim in text 1? text 1 elena marquez and colleagues observed seven captive bottlenose dolphins ( tursiops truncatus ) outfitted with experimental suction-cup data loggers. while fitting each dolphin, the researchers noticed that some dolphins without loggers would nudge a logger on another dolphin with their rostrums until the device detached. the researchers proposed that this behavior could indicate dolphins helping one another without any benefit to the helpers. text 2 it is appealing to interpret animals as deliberately assisting one another when we see them removing equipment from each other, particularly in species already known to engage in other forms of cooperation. yet it can be difficult to rule out the possibility that individuals are simply intrigued by the unfamiliar object, nosing or batting at it until it eventually comes loose.

preview: Text 1 Cultured meat is animal tissue grown from cells in a bioreactor as an alternative to conventional livestock products. Life-cycle ass…
searchText: based on the texts, how would the author of text 1 most likely respond to the study findings mentioned in text 2? text 1 cultured meat is animal tissue grown from cells in a bioreactor as an alternative to conventional livestock products. life-cycle assessments of its production generally agree that its environmental footprint is lower than that of beef and comparable to that of poultry. the expense of culture media, however, currently limits its availability to a handful of wealthy markets. recognizing that cost reductions would broaden access, bioengineers are testing solutions such as cheaper plant-derived growth-factor mixtures. text 2 beef production is a major driver of tropical deforestation, and a study by anika lindgren and her team estimated that replacing 25% of global beef consumption with cultured beef over the next forty years would reduce forest clearance for cattle by roughly 60%. however, that shift would barely change overall agricultural land use, since cropland once devoted to feed grains would be redirected toward growing crops for the culture-media supply chain.

preview: Text 1 Compelling fiction often unsettles its readers by violating familiar narrative expectations, but the production of children's litera…
searchText: based on the texts, how would the scholars mentioned in text 2 most likely respond to the underlined claim in text 1? text 1 compelling fiction often unsettles its readers by violating familiar narrative expectations, but the production of children's literature—novels, picture books, and graphic stories marketed for school-age readers—typically depends on broad consensus among authors, editors, parents, and educators about a book's themes and lessons. a children's book that fails to accommodate every gatekeeper by being sufficiently safe and unchallenging almost always provokes objection. text 2 children's literature is most often delivered to young readers through institutions, such as schools and libraries, whose primary missions concern academic instruction or general public service rather than aesthetic exploration. some scholars of children's literature therefore observe that norm-defying books not carefully framed for their context—through teacher introductions, reading guides, or curated displays that prepare readers to engage thoughtfully with difficult ideas—tend to draw harsher complaints than equally provocative titles encountered in less institutional settings, such as a parent's recommendation at home.

preview: Text 1 Most vertebrates can replace some kinds of damaged tissue, such as skin. But when an axolotl loses a limb, it grows a new one, compl…
searchText: based on the texts, what would the author of text 2 most likely say about text 1's characterization of the discovery involving wnt? text 1 most vertebrates can replace some kinds of damaged tissue, such as skin. but when an axolotl loses a limb, it grows a new one, complete with bones, muscles, and nerves. researchers are studying this remarkable ability in part to understand why humans, by comparison, regenerate so poorly, and they're making promising progress. one especially exciting finding is that both humans and axolotls produce a regenerative signaling protein called wnt. text 2 when karen echeverri and her team reported that axolotls, like humans, rely on wnt signaling during tissue repair, it generated enthusiasm in the press. as the team itself cautioned, however, the protein likely operates very differently in humans than it does in axolotls. echeverri has compared axolotl wnt to a conductor that coordinates a chorus of regenerative genes; the comparable role wnt may or may not play in humans remains entirely uncharacterized.

preview: Text 1 Chemically analyzing bulk lake-sediment samples from a Greenland coring site, Bryn Halverson et al. detected a brief but pronounced…
searchText: based on the texts, halverson et al. (text 1) and tanaka et al. (text 2) would most likely disagree about the answer to which question about the portion of the greenland core corresponding to 14,000 bp? text 1 chemically analyzing bulk lake-sediment samples from a greenland coring site, bryn halverson et al. detected a brief but pronounced enrichment of vanadium (v) in a layer corresponding to roughly 14,000 years before present (bp). because v is mobilized chiefly by sustained reducing conditions in lake bottom waters, halverson et al. concluded that the lake briefly became strongly anoxic at that time, then returned to its earlier oxygenated state. text 2 reiko tanaka et al. reanalyzed the same greenland core. because bulk chemical analysis can mask spatial heterogeneity, tanaka et al. supplemented it with high-resolution synchrotron imaging, which revealed thin, isolated layers of pyrite—an iron sulfide and a well-known v host—at the 14,000 bp horizon, embedded around tiny burrows made by sediment-dwelling worms. the team contends that pore waters could have moved through these burrows, depositing v into the pyrite long after the corresponding sediments were laid down.

preview: Text 1 Biologists have long puzzled over how thousands of distinct soil-microbe species can coexist within a single handful of forest soil,…
searchText: based on the texts, how would anand and colleagues (text 2) most likely respond to the "conventional wisdom" discussed in text 1? text 1 biologists have long puzzled over how thousands of distinct soil-microbe species can coexist within a single handful of forest soil, all drawing on the same pool of carbon and nitrogen. according to conventional wisdom , one species ought to dominate after outcompeting the rest. why, then, do so many persist? decades of effort have yet to produce a satisfying explanation. text 2 microbial ecologist devon anand and colleagues have linked soil-microbe diversity to the physical structure of the soil itself. because individual cells are so small relative to the gaps between soil grains, microbes inhabit countless tiny, semi-isolated pore spaces, each effectively functioning as its own miniature habitat. the team argues that, as a result, neighboring species rarely interact directly enough to compete for the same resources, making head-to-head competition among soil microbes far less common than ecologists had previously assumed.

preview: Text 1 Herman Melville's 1857 novel The Confidence-Man stands apart from his other major works. His earlier novels build tightly around ext…
searchText: based on the texts, how would the author of text 2 most likely respond to the assessment of the confidence-man presented in text 1? text 1 herman melville's 1857 novel the confidence-man stands apart from his other major works. his earlier novels build tightly around extended seafaring adventures and probe their characters' inner lives at length, whereas the confidence-man proceeds through a loosely linked series of conversations on a mississippi steamboat and renders its characters' minds far more obliquely. melville himself sometimes treated the novel as a minor experiment, once describing it dismissively as "a curiosity." text 2 like melville's other great novels, the confidence-man dramatizes how people negotiate trust in a world full of strangers. like those earlier works, it scrutinizes the deceptions people enact for themselves and for one another. though its tone is sharper, more sardonic—the comedy of a sustained con rather than the grandeur of a hunted whale—this seeming "curiosity" engages no less seriously with the moral preoccupations that animate the handful of melville's novels now regarded as classics.

preview: Text 1 The Late Devonian mass extinction is sometimes attributed solely to widespread oceanic anoxia. Other paleontologists argue that mass…
searchText: based on the texts, how would briceño's team (text 2) most likely respond to the argument in the underlined portion of text 1? text 1 the late devonian mass extinction is sometimes attributed solely to widespread oceanic anoxia. other paleontologists argue that massive eruptions of the viluy traps volcanic province were the true cause, since the extinctions occurred during an extended period of viluy eruption that initially flooded the atmosphere with climate-altering gases. these dissenters note that several other mass extinctions coincide with large volcanic provinces, whereas the link between extinction and anoxia events is far less consistent. text 2 in a 2022 study, paleoceanographer tomás briceño and his colleagues analyzed deep-sea sediment cores and modeled atmospheric chemistry across the late devonian. the team concluded that viluy traps gases did indeed alter global climate before the main extinction pulse, but that climate had largely stabilized well before the extinctions intensified—extinctions that instead align tightly with peak oceanic anoxia recorded in the cores.

preview: Text 1 When the first commercial telephone exchange opened in New Haven in 1878 with just twenty-one subscribers, many of those subscribers…
searchText: based on the texts, the author of text 2 would most likely agree with which statement about the description of the first telephone exchange in text 1? text 1 when the first commercial telephone exchange opened in new haven in 1878 with just twenty-one subscribers, many of those subscribers, completely unaccustomed to instantly transmitted speech, were reportedly so startled by the disembodied voices coming from the wooden handsets that they refused at first to speak back, only listening in baffled silence. this moment marks a profound cultural rupture: a new way of communicating across distance had arrived with that switchboard, and nothing about social life would ever be the same. text 2 the fact that no contemporary newspaper account from 1878 actually describes such a paralyzed silence has not prevented the anecdote from becoming a staple in popular histories of the telephone, even those written by communications scholars. but that pattern itself is highly revealing. our conviction that the arrival of the telephone fundamentally reorganized social life is so deep that we have invented and keep retelling a founding myth dividing communication history into a (naive) "before" and a (modern) "after."

preview: Text 1 In 1924, scholar Ezra K. Maxwell challenged the prevailing view that the seventeenth-century play The Witch of Edmonton was the join…
searchText: based on the texts, both maxwell in text 1 and the scholars in text 2 would most likely agree with which statement? text 1 in 1924, scholar ezra k. maxwell challenged the prevailing view that the seventeenth-century play the witch of edmonton was the joint work of william rowley and thomas dekker alone. maxwell felt rowley's contributions to the play were unmistakable—rowley had a distinctive habit of metrical irregularity in his other works, and lines exhibiting that habit were regarded as sufficient proof of his hand. but for the lines not deemed to be by rowley, maxwell felt that their handling of comic peasant speech indicated that their author was not dekker but john ford. text 2 scholars have generally accepted the witch of edmonton as the work of rowley, dekker, and ford since the 1980s, and the play now appears in all major editions of early modern english drama. although critics disagree about the precise division of labor, it is broadly held, on stylistic grounds, that rowley wrote most of the comic scenes, dekker most of the domestic-tragedy material, and ford the witch-related subplot.

searchText: based on the texts, if ruiz and lavoie (text 1) and lange et al. (text 2) were aware of both sets of findings, they would most likely agree with which statement? text 1 aged cheese, particularly parmigiano-reggiano, is celebrated for its intense savory taste. that savory quality—part of what scientists describe as the umami sensation—has been the subject of detailed chemical study since umami receptors were identified in the 2000s. in 2010, to characterize the savory profile of aged cheese, food scientists ana ruiz and henri lavoie prepared simplified extracts containing free glutamate and a small set of nucleotides, and two panels of italian and french tasters described the resulting flavor in closely matching terms. text 2 a 2023 experiment by wiebke lange et al. extended the chemistry of aged cheese well beyond simple glutamate. the team first offered tasters a mixture of low-molecular-weight compounds that they found tasted thin and less complex than actual parmigiano-reggiano. further fractionation of the cheese revealed several short peptides that enhanced savory taste while also intensifying the perception of saltiness and richness. the team then assembled a blend of 40 chemical compounds that successfully reproduced the flavor of aged cheese.

preview: Text 1 The Australian rainforest—once an unbroken belt of moist forest—began to retreat sharply about 4,000 years ago. Shifts in El Niño ac…
searchText: based on the texts, how would hollister (text 2) most likely respond to the discussion in text 1? text 1 the australian rainforest—once an unbroken belt of moist forest—began to retreat sharply about 4,000 years ago. shifts in el niño activity that affected rainfall have been proposed as a cause of the contraction, but archaeologist lewis trent also attributes the change to aboriginal peoples. he cites their growing reliance on landscape burning as a factor in the drying of the region: fires set by these communities thinned the forest canopy, prompting feedbacks that hastened the conversion of rainforest to open woodland. text 2 research by maeve hollister et al. challenges the idea that aboriginal peoples contributed to the australian rainforest's retreat. using a high-resolution pollen-vegetation model, the team concluded that the most pronounced phase of rainforest decline occurred 600 years earlier than previously assumed. that timing suggests that aboriginal peoples did not accelerate forest loss but, in fact, may have helped slow it through practices (such as controlled mosaic burning) that maintained pockets of rainforest within a drier landscape.

preview: Text 1 Conventional wisdom in economic history has long claimed that systems of money evolved in clear stages, beginning with barter exchan…
searchText: based on the texts, how would wu and halvorsen (text 2) most likely respond to the "conventional wisdom" presented in text 1? text 1 conventional wisdom in economic history has long claimed that systems of money evolved in clear stages, beginning with barter exchange among small face-to-face communities. the growth of trade about 5,000 years ago is said to have driven the emergence of commodity money, followed by metal coinage, and finally modern paper and electronic currencies issued by states. text 2 in a 2022 book, economic anthropologist mei lin wu and historian theo halvorsen argue that human societies have always relied on a flexible mix of credit, gift, and tribute arrangements rather than progressing through fixed monetary stages. the authors cite evidence that as early as 4,000 years ago some mesopotamian communities used elaborate ledger-based credit systems alongside, and not after, occasional barter, with shifts between these arrangements occurring at the level of individual households.

preview: Text 1 In 2009, a team led by Daniel Renfrew analyzed sweet potato remains discovered in an archaeological deposit on Mangaia, Cook Islands…
searchText: based on the texts, how would the author of text 2 most likely respond to the underlined claim in text 1? text 1 in 2009, a team led by daniel renfrew analyzed sweet potato remains discovered in an archaeological deposit on mangaia, cook islands, dating them to 1180–1260 ce—over two centuries before european voyages reached the central pacific, bringing crops from the americas. renfrew also found that the mangaia sweet potatoes carried a distinctive chloroplast haplotype shared with ancient varieties grown along the andean coast. thus, polynesian voyagers, not later europeans, probably first introduced sweet potatoes to the islands of the central pacific. text 2 a european research group later weakened the case for an andean origin for the mangaia sweet potatoes by confirming that renfrew's chloroplast haplotype is in fact found in sweet potato varieties from many parts of the world. more recently, however, a team led by anaïs ramos-vidal found that polynesian sweet potato varieties and andean varieties share several nuclear dna markers that caribbean and mesoamerican varieties lack. thus, the preponderance of evidence now favors a polynesian-andean exchange.

preview: Text 1 Astronomer Ravi Subramanian and colleagues examined the atmospheres of five hot Neptunes—Neptune-sized exoplanets that orbit unusual…
searchText: based on the texts, how would vargas and tomori (text 2) most likely characterize the conclusion presented in text 1? text 1 astronomer ravi subramanian and colleagues examined the atmospheres of five hot neptunes—neptune-sized exoplanets that orbit unusually close to their host stars—in order to constrain the composition of the planets' deep interiors. studying infrared spectral lines from the atmospheres, the team reported that traces of elements such as rubidium and cesium support the presence of buried metallic-hydrogen mantles like those once predicted for warm jovian worlds. text 2 past atmospheric studies of hot neptunes have concluded that some such planets contain metallic-hydrogen mantles. geophysicist helena vargas and astronomer akira tomori argue that those studies place too much weight on atmospheric traces of rubidium and other individual elements as proxies for specific interior structures. the studies, they note, fail to adequately consider that different deep-interior compounds, made up of various ratios of those elements, and even kinds of high-pressure phases not seen in our own solar system, could produce indistinguishable atmospheric signatures.

preview: Text 1 What factors shape the long-term diversity of plant species in a meadow? Some theorists have argued that founding diversity is a maj…
searchText: based on the texts, how would almeida and colleagues (text 2) most likely describe the view of the theorists presented in text 1? text 1 what factors shape the long-term diversity of plant species in a meadow? some theorists have argued that founding diversity is a major driver of how diverse a meadow community eventually becomes: differences in the diversity of grasslands occupying otherwise comparable sites, in this view, are strongly influenced by the number of plant species established in those sites at the very start. text 2 in 2015, a team of researchers led by ecologist júlia almeida established replicate experimental meadows on cleared farmland in southern brazil. they seeded some plots with a richly diverse mix of native grasses and wildflowers and others with only a single grass species, then allowed the plots to develop without further intervention. over the next five years, almeida and colleagues regularly sampled plant species in the plots, finding—contrary to their expectations—that by the end of the study there was almost no difference in plant species diversity between the two types of plots.

preview: Text 1 For decades, paleontologists have placed the disappearance of the saiga antelope from much of central Eurasia at roughly 5 thousand…
searchText: which choice best describes how text 1 and text 2 relate to each other? text 1 for decades, paleontologists have placed the disappearance of the saiga antelope from much of central eurasia at roughly 5 thousand years (kyr) ago, based on bone records. in 2022, however, sediment cores examined by inessa polzin and colleagues yielded saiga environmental dna in layers dated to only 2 kyr ago. to reconcile this with the bone record, theodore brace and yuri lenkov proposed that frigid steppe winds could preserve saiga remains intact above ground for thousands of years, allowing slow leakage of dna into the soil. text 2 polzin and her colleagues acknowledge that environmental dna derives from carcasses as well as living animals, but for dna to leak from remains over millennia, those remains must persist on the surface for the entire span. in the open steppe, however, foxes, wolves, and rapid freeze-thaw cycles tend to disassemble any large carcass within a century or two.

preview: Text 1 Like the painter Joaquín Sorolla before her, María Blanchard's canvases include sun-drenched coastal scenes whose loose brushwork an…
searchText: based on the texts, how would mendoza (text 2) most likely respond to the underlined claim in text 1? text 1 like the painter joaquín sorolla before her, maría blanchard's canvases include sun-drenched coastal scenes whose loose brushwork and high-keyed palette so closely echo sorolla's earlier works that the two artists can momentarily seem interchangeable. given the many other parallels between them in subject and composition, these scenes suggest a direct artistic debt owed by blanchard to sorolla. text 2 critic helena mendoza cautions that perceived stylistic affinities should not be confused with documented influence. sorolla's luminous mediterranean idiom belonged to a broad early twentieth-century movement that many artists, blanchard included, encountered through exhibitions, journals, and shared teachers. without letters, diaries, or interviews attesting that blanchard studied sorolla in particular, mendoza argues, attributing her coastal works specifically to his influence risks overstating one channel of transmission.

preview: Text 1 Submerged coral terraces off the northern coast of Madagascar preserve a record of past sea levels, but assigning ages to individual…
searchText: based on the texts, how would the researchers in text 2 most likely respond to the overall account of dating in text 1? text 1 submerged coral terraces off the northern coast of madagascar preserve a record of past sea levels, but assigning ages to individual terrace layers has long been challenging. strong surface currents have repeatedly disturbed the shallowest layers, mixing coral fragments from different periods, and dates inferred from associated sediments are unreliable because storms can shift those sediments by hundreds of meters. for decades, scientists working in this environment have therefore had to attach broad and uncertain age ranges to each terrace. text 2 a team of marine geologists used a refined uranium-thorium dating technique to reevaluate the ages of coral fragments from cores drilled deep into the madagascar terraces. the technique relies on isotopic ratios measured directly from undisturbed, mineralogically intact aragonite within the coral skeleton itself. the researchers argue that this approach avoids the dating errors that arise from analyzing surface samples or associated sediments and can yield ages precise enough to distinguish individual terrace layers.

preview: Text 1 Patent records are often touted as a comprehensive register of nineteenth-century inventors, especially women inventors whose contri…
searchText: based on the texts, how would whitfield (text 2) most likely respond to the characterization of patent records offered in text 1? text 1 patent records are often touted as a comprehensive register of nineteenth-century inventors, especially women inventors whose contributions are otherwise hard to recover. because patents list the applicant by name and date, scholars treat them as a reliable starting point for studies of innovation in industries from textiles to telegraphy. text 2 historian lucia whitfield warns against equating the patent record with the actual landscape of nineteenth-century invention. patenting required filing fees, travel to government offices, and, in many jurisdictions, the legal capacity to hold property in one's own name—requirements that excluded most married women and many working-class women regardless of how prolific they were as inventors. a name-based search of the patent ledger, whitfield argues, will systematically underrepresent precisely the groups it appears to document.

preview: DNA studies have led researchers to suggest that giant pandas are most closely related to the group that includes modern bears. But studies…
searchText: which choice best describes the function of the underlined sentence? dna studies have led researchers to suggest that giant pandas are most closely related to the group that includes modern bears. but studies of fossils have suggested instead that pandas are most closely related to other groups, such as the one that contains modern raccoons. however, many of these fossil studies have relied on small or fragmentary samples. for a 2020 investigation, biologist wei lin and colleagues examined more than 800 mammal fossils from sites across asia. from this large data set, they found clear agreement with the results of the dna studies.

preview: Archaeologists have found a roughly 9,000-year-old stone tool from a member of an early human community on a small island off the coast of…
searchText: which choice best states the function of the underlined portion in the text as a whole? archaeologists have found a roughly 9,000-year-old stone tool from a member of an early human community on a small island off the coast of greece, suggesting that these communities reached more distant locations than earlier evidence indicated. before the discovery, these communities were thought to have stayed close to the mainland, traveling only short distances along sheltered coastlines , but the discovery of the tool on the island suggests that they may have crossed open sea over significant distances as well.

preview: Zora Neale Hurston's novel Their Eyes Were Watching God (1937) is among the first major novels by a Black woman to draw extensively on Sout…
searchText: which choice best states the main purpose of the text? zora neale hurston's novel their eyes were watching god (1937) is among the first major novels by a black woman to draw extensively on southern folk dialect. the book tells the story of janie, a black woman in florida who searches for love and self-knowledge across three marriages. janie's story reflects the experiences of many black women in the rural south in the early twentieth century. their eyes were watching god thus shows how hurston used fiction to portray the inner lives of black southern women, which she continued to do throughout her career.

preview: In many urban environments, strips of grass and shrubs are kept along roadways to filter rainwater runoff, but it has been unclear what eff…
searchText: which choice best states the main purpose of the text? in many urban environments, strips of grass and shrubs are kept along roadways to filter rainwater runoff, but it has been unclear what effects these strips may have on local insect diversity. to investigate, entomologist karim ahmadi and colleagues studied parks in toronto, comparing the species richness of strips with native plantings to that of strips with only mown grass. ahmadi and colleagues found that species richness was significantly higher in the native-plant strips, a finding the researchers attribute to the role native flowers play in providing nectar and shelter for insects.

preview: Were whales always sea creatures? Paleontologist Maria Reyes and her team argue that whales had ancestors that walked on land but that thos…
searchText: which choice best describes the function of the underlined sentence in the text as a whole? were whales always sea creatures? paleontologist maria reyes and her team argue that whales had ancestors that walked on land but that those ancestors gradually shifted to a fully aquatic life over tens of millions of years. after examining various fossil skeletons and genetic information, the researchers concluded that whale ancestors developed streamlined bodies, fluked tails, and modified ear bones better suited to underwater life than to land. thus, environmental conditions might have driven whales from land to sea.

preview: In 1855, a Cherokee surveyor named Sequoyah Tahlonteeskee drew a finely detailed map of the Arkansas River basin. This work demonstrates a…
searchText: which choice best describes the function of the underlined sentence in the text as a whole? in 1855, a cherokee surveyor named sequoyah tahlonteeskee drew a finely detailed map of the arkansas river basin. this work demonstrates a great deal of geographic knowledge, as the map features precise names of tributaries and ridges, as well as the first known sketch of the basin's seasonal floodplain. the map is especially notable because tahlonteeskee also marked the locations of several indigenous communities living in the region.

preview: The following text is adapted from a 2018 short story. The narrator, who is in middle school, is sitting on a bench at the mall. I just sit…
searchText: which choice best states the main purpose of the text? the following text is adapted from a 2018 short story. the narrator, who is in middle school, is sitting on a bench at the mall. i just sit here to watch the people coming out of the photo booth. see, the booth across the way has this little curtain—like nothing more than a sheet, really—and they got those flashes that make you blink real hard and look like you just walked into the sun, so every group comes out blinking and squinting and shoving each other, looking all goofy. and trust me, there ain't nothing funnier than that. so i just sit there for a bit like it's some kind of show: the blinking bunch , starring photo-booth victim one through twenty.

preview: The following text is adapted from a 2021 novel. Mira is a teenager who lives with her father, Theo, and her grandmother, Lina, who is a po…
searchText: which choice best states the function of the underlined sentence in the text as a whole? the following text is adapted from a 2021 novel. mira is a teenager who lives with her father, theo, and her grandmother, lina, who is a potter. lina finished the vase in the gray hour of a morning—it was just before dawn when mira heard the kiln door swing open. she lay in the dimness of her room and listened to her grandmother's footsteps moving down the hallway between her [grandmother] and theo's room. there was a low hum pulsing through the wooden floor in a kind of melody, and that was how mira knew the vase was done. lina's feet were humming the news.

preview: In the past, scholars who wanted to study Harriet Tubman's letters and personal records had to travel to the National Archives in Washingto…
searchText: which choice best describes the function of the underlined portion in the text as a whole? in the past, scholars who wanted to study harriet tubman's letters and personal records had to travel to the national archives in washington, dc, to view them on microfiche (small sheets of film containing tiny reproductions of documents) . but traveling to the archives often added time and expense to a research project. now, by visiting the archives' website, scholars can access digitized versions of tubman's papers without leaving home.

preview: Some fish species don't watch over their own young. Instead, adult females release their eggs near other fishes' nests, mixing them with an…
searchText: which choice best describes the function of the underlined sentence in the text as a whole? some fish species don't watch over their own young. instead, adult females release their eggs near other fishes' nests, mixing them with another species' own eggs. female cuckoo catfish have been observed quickly releasing eggs into the nests of mouthbrooding cichlids while those fish are gathering their own eggs. after the eggs hatch, the noncatfish parents typically tend to the catfish young as if they were their own, even though the catfish look very different from the other fry.

preview: Renata Okafor is a researcher who studies pollinator corridors. A pollinator corridor is a strip of land between farms or fields where wild…
searchText: which choice best describes the text's overall structure? renata okafor is a researcher who studies pollinator corridors. a pollinator corridor is a strip of land between farms or fields where wildflowers and native plants are encouraged to grow. these plants attract bees, butterflies, and other pollinators. pollinator corridors help support healthy crop production (how flowering crops are pollinated and made fruitful) in farming areas. this benefit is one reason okafor is interested in pollinator corridors along soybean fields in the central united states.

preview: Animals use many sounds to communicate with one another. Such uses include warning of danger, attracting mates, and marking territory. For…
searchText: which choice best states the main purpose of the text? animals use many sounds to communicate with one another. such uses include warning of danger, attracting mates, and marking territory. for a long time, people thought that complex vocal communication was unique to mammals and birds. frogs and other animals, though, have shown that complex calls don't require fur or feathers. imaginatively, certain frog species mix high and low notes to indicate the size of an approaching predator. coqui frogs also get expressive. they change the pitch of their calls when staking out a new perch.

preview: The following text is adapted from a 2019 novel. More than three years ago my aunt bought a grandfather clock from a neighbor who was movin…
searchText: which choice best states the main purpose of the text? the following text is adapted from a 2019 novel. more than three years ago my aunt bought a grandfather clock from a neighbor who was moving overseas. aunt pia gave it to my sister and me for our birthdays. i had to act really pleased because it was such a serious gift, but i pretty much disliked the thing from the moment it was wheeled into the corner of our living room, which is right next to the doorway to my bedroom. the clock loomed over me. it was like a sentry on watch. it wanted me to stand at attention.

preview: Dale Chihuly is known worldwide for his glass sculptures of sea forms. These sculptures are large and tend not to depict the entire creatur…
searchText: which choice best describes the overall structure of the text? dale chihuly is known worldwide for his glass sculptures of sea forms. these sculptures are large and tend not to depict the entire creature. instead, chihuly focuses on just the curling shapes of tentacles and shells, leaving any suggestion of a face out of view. while chihuly's sculptures are rooted in glassblowing techniques that developed in italy, the artist uses bold american color choices and gestures that make his work instantly recognizable.

preview: Many radio programs from the 1930s have been lost. These losses include several programs created by the first wave of Latina radio hosts. W…
