searchText: which choice completes the text with the most logical transition? the more intricate and varied a bird species' nest-building behavior, the larger and more metabolically costly its forebrain region tends to be. ______ blank from an evolutionary perspective, species whose nesting routines are uniform should devote fewer resources to developing and sustaining forebrain tissue. the distinct nest-building strategies of weaverbirds provide a rich opportunity to test this hypothesis.

preview: Acclaimed nature documentarian Aroha Tane regards the field notebooks she keeps while observing wildlife in remote habitats to be a rewardi…
searchText: which choice completes the text with the most logical transition? acclaimed nature documentarian aroha tane regards the field notebooks she keeps while observing wildlife in remote habitats to be a rewarding pursuit in their own right. ______ blank tane confesses that she finds the note-taking phase of her fieldwork just as captivating and absorbing as filming the animals themselves.

preview: "Greenwashing"—labeling products as environmentally friendly under the misleading impression that those products are genuinely sustainable—…
searchText: which choice completes the text with the most logical transition? "greenwashing"—labeling products as environmentally friendly under the misleading impression that those products are genuinely sustainable—ultimately does more damage than good. greenwashed products, such as plastic bottles marketed as "eco-friendly," can lead consumers to discard more single-use packaging, increasing landfill waste. ______ blank greenwashed marketing campaigns can erode public trust in legitimately sustainable brands.

preview: A bombardier beetle stores hydroquinone and hydrogen peroxide in separate chambers within its abdomen, then forces these compounds together…
searchText: which choice completes the text with the most logical transition? a bombardier beetle stores hydroquinone and hydrogen peroxide in separate chambers within its abdomen, then forces these compounds together with catalytic enzymes to trigger a violent exothermic reaction that ejects a hot, noxious spray. ______ blank when the beetle closes its valves and isolates the chambers, the reaction—and the spray—immediately stop.

preview: With their long, blade-like leaves and tall flowering spikes, the plants Phormium tenax (New Zealand) and Yucca filamentosa (United States)…
searchText: which choice completes the text with the most logical transition? with their long, blade-like leaves and tall flowering spikes, the plants phormium tenax (new zealand) and yucca filamentosa (united states) may appear nearly identical to a casual observer. botanist hira manaaki and her colleagues, ______ blank can determine from each plant's flower structure that phormium belongs to the lily family, while yucca belongs to the asparagus family.

preview: Prior to Oregon's 1912 referendum to extend voting rights to women, organizers throughout the state distributed printed cookbooks to advanc…
searchText: which choice completes the text with the most logical transition? prior to oregon's 1912 referendum to extend voting rights to women, organizers throughout the state distributed printed cookbooks to advance the suffrage cause. in portland, the college equal suffrage league compiled the votes for women cook book for sale at civic gatherings. ______ blank in salem, activist marguerite whitmore, who managed one of oregon's most popular bakeries, assembled the suffrage recipes booklet for her customers.

preview: Scholar Yuki Tanaka observes that the poet Marianne Moore "never regarded a poem as finished," routinely revising her verses years after th…
searchText: which choice completes the text with the most logical transition? scholar yuki tanaka observes that the poet marianne moore "never regarded a poem as finished," routinely revising her verses years after their original appearance in print. however, the divergences between the 1924 first edition and the 1951 collected edition of her poem poetry are striking, even by moore's standards; ______ blank some scholars treat the two versions as fundamentally separate works.

preview: In 1829, while in exile in Paris, Polish poet Adam Mickiewicz drafted an open letter celebrating the political maturity of independent Euro…
searchText: which choice completes the text with the most logical transition? in 1829, while in exile in paris, polish poet adam mickiewicz drafted an open letter celebrating the political maturity of independent european nations and expressing hope that the partitioned regions of poland might someday be reunited. the letter was addressed to a polish émigré bookseller, karol forster; ______ blank though, mickiewicz's aim was to nudge sympathetic western politicians into supporting polish self-rule.

preview: Hyperloop systems propose to move passenger pods at near-supersonic speeds through low-pressure tubes, drastically cutting intercity travel…
searchText: which choice completes the text with the most logical transition? hyperloop systems propose to move passenger pods at near-supersonic speeds through low-pressure tubes, drastically cutting intercity travel times. although hyperloop advocates in the us have spent more than a decade promising that the technology would soon crisscross the country, no commercial passenger system has opened. ______ blank the only operational hyperloop-style passenger tests so far have been short demonstration runs, none longer than 500 meters.

preview: A 2019 study of early second-language learners examined the role of cognate similarity—the closeness of a word's form in two languages—in v…
searchText: which choice completes the text with the most logical transition? a 2019 study of early second-language learners examined the role of cognate similarity—the closeness of a word's form in two languages—in vocabulary acquisition. the study found that the greater the cognate similarity of a target word, the more likely learners were to acquire it. ______ blank the correlation between acquisition and cognate similarity was weaker than that between acquisition and a separate factor studied: classroom exposure frequency.

preview: While printing the first issues of the colony of Maryland's official gazette in the 1740s, Jonas Green—then a young Annapolis printer—took…
searchText: which choice completes the text with the most logical transition? while printing the first issues of the colony of maryland's official gazette in the 1740s, jonas green—then a young annapolis printer—took several steps to discourage forgery, such as folding sheets to create distinctive watermarks and mixing custom batches of ink to deter substitution. ______ blank he stamped each issue with intricate hand-cut woodblock ornaments that were difficult for counterfeiters to reproduce.

preview: Historically, most senior judges on national supreme courts have shared similar demographic backgrounds, but a new wave of justices is broa…
searchText: which choice completes the text with the most logical transition? historically, most senior judges on national supreme courts have shared similar demographic backgrounds, but a new wave of justices is broadening representation. bolivia's silvia salame served as the first indigenous-identifying justice to sit on her country's supreme court of justice in 2007, ______ blank and senegal's aïssata tall sall was appointed to a comparable senior judicial role in 2020.

preview: To pull off a quintuple twist on the floor, a gymnast must rotate around the vertical axis five full times before landing, a feat once cons…
searchText: which choice completes the text with the most logical transition? to pull off a quintuple twist on the floor, a gymnast must rotate around the vertical axis five full times before landing, a feat once considered impossible. ______ blank in 2024, when 19-year-old mai tran landed the first quintuple twist—long viewed as the most demanding twisting skill—in a senior international competition, the crowd erupted in disbelief.

preview: The international meter, the global standard for length first defined by physical artifact in 1799, was originally measured against a plati…
searchText: which choice completes the text with the most logical transition? the international meter, the global standard for length first defined by physical artifact in 1799, was originally measured against a platinum-iridium bar stored in paris. ______ blank as decades passed, finer instruments revealed that the bar's length subtly shifted with temperature and handling, yet the artifact remained the official reference; it wasn't until 1983 that, spurred by precise atomic measurements, the meter was officially redefined—based now on the speed of light in vacuum.

preview: When viewers first encounter Edgar Mueller's The Crevasse , a vivid chalk drawing on a Dublin sidewalk, many step back instinctively, sure…
searchText: which choice completes the text with the most logical transition? when viewers first encounter edgar mueller's the crevasse , a vivid chalk drawing on a dublin sidewalk, many step back instinctively, sure that the pavement has split open into a deep, jagged chasm. ______ blank by carefully applying the principles of perspective, mueller has produced only the illusion of depth.

preview: Locke believed that the mind at birth is a blank slate, with all knowledge arriving through sensory experience. In his view, no innate prin…
searchText: which choice completes the text with the most logical transition? locke believed that the mind at birth is a blank slate, with all knowledge arriving through sensory experience. in his view, no innate principles exist; rather, ideas are built up from impressions of the world. leibniz disagreed, arguing that some structures—such as basic logical and mathematical truths—must be innate to the mind itself; ______ blank certain ideas precede and shape any experience of the world.

preview: In Mary Oliver's Upstream —where, early in the book, the author lingers in awe over the iridescent shimmer of a single trout but later mour…
searchText: which choice completes the text with the most logical transition? in mary oliver's upstream —where, early in the book, the author lingers in awe over the iridescent shimmer of a single trout but later mourns watching that same trout devoured by a heron she has been admiring for pages, and where she returns again and again to scenes in which loveliness and brutality occupy the same instant—oliver does not so much resolve the contradictions of the natural world as patiently inhabit them, turning each tension over in her hands without flinching. ______ blank nature's quiet beauty and indifferent violence prove, for oliver, two sides of the same coin.

preview: One of the central insights of modern soil science is that the microbial community surrounding a plant's roots—the rhizosphere—exerts a pro…
searchText: which choice completes the text with the most logical transition? one of the central insights of modern soil science is that the microbial community surrounding a plant's roots—the rhizosphere—exerts a profound influence on the plant's ability to absorb nutrients from the surrounding earth. ______ blank instead of evaluating crop varieties solely by their genetic traits, agronomists are increasingly assessing how those varieties interact with the diverse microbes that colonize their root systems.

preview: Looking back at the early 1900s, given the foundational work on radioactivity that had already been published by Henri Becquerel and the Cu…
searchText: which choice completes the text with the most logical transition? looking back at the early 1900s, given the foundational work on radioactivity that had already been published by henri becquerel and the curies, the discovery of the atomic nucleus by an experimentalist of ernest rutherford's caliber was, in many respects, a natural next step. it may not have been mere chance, ______ blank that researchers in several european laboratories were converging on similar conclusions during the same decade. the history of physics offers many such cases in which a major insight emerges from multiple investigators working independently along parallel lines.

preview: Oceanographers Naoto Hirano and Yuka Sato have argued that the unusually warm seawater circulating through the hydrothermal vents along the…
searchText: which choice completes the text with the most logical transition? oceanographers naoto hirano and yuka sato have argued that the unusually warm seawater circulating through the hydrothermal vents along the south pacific's petit-spot volcanoes is drawn from depths far greater than those typically associated with such systems. the vents tap reservoirs nearly 500 meters below the seafloor—an unusual figure that marks these vents as clear outliers; ______ blank the vast majority of hydrothermal vents around the world draw on reservoirs less than 100 meters deep.

preview: During the cold months, many small mammals enter a state of torpor, in which their metabolic rates plummet and body temperatures drop to co…
searchText: which choice completes the text with the most logical transition? during the cold months, many small mammals enter a state of torpor, in which their metabolic rates plummet and body temperatures drop to conserve energy. in most species, this hibernation-like dormancy lasts continuously for weeks at a time. ______ blank the arctic ground squirrel urocitellus parryii regularly interrupts its torpor with brief, energetically costly arousals during which its body temperature returns nearly to normal.

preview: Long horizontal swaths of land set aside on city blocks for cultivation are known as allotment gardens, a term borrowed from late nineteent…
searchText: which choice completes the text with the most logical transition? long horizontal swaths of land set aside on city blocks for cultivation are known as allotment gardens, a term borrowed from late nineteenth-century british policy. as urban allotments have transitioned from emergency food production to a leisure pursuit, contemporary allotments are seldom managed strictly for caloric output. ______ blank today's plot holders tend to prioritize variety and aesthetics, devoting space to ornamental flowers or unusual heirloom vegetables that hold cultural rather than nutritional value.

preview: In his novel sequence The Border Trilogy , Cormac McCarthy weaves the lives of his young protagonists into larger currents of twentieth-cen…
searchText: which choice completes the text with the most logical transition? in his novel sequence the border trilogy , cormac mccarthy weaves the lives of his young protagonists into larger currents of twentieth-century north american history. he frames john grady cole's 1949 ride into mexico against the slow collapse of the cattle-ranching economy of the american southwest. ______ blank mccarthy positions billy parham's later wanderings against the shifting boundary politics and modernization of the postwar borderlands.

preview: Visitors to Aiko Tezuka's textile installations are often drawn in by the swirling, weather-like patterns that her tapestries seem to proje…
searchText: which choice completes the text with the most logical transition? visitors to aiko tezuka's textile installations are often drawn in by the swirling, weather-like patterns that her tapestries seem to project, and many find themselves imagining storm fronts sweeping across alien skies. ______ blank it's the artist's wall label that pulls them back into the studio, revealing that each tapestry is woven entirely from unraveled department-store curtains.

preview: In her 2019 essay "Cartographies of Sound," musicologist Iris Halberg reflects on a composition that has shaped her thinking: Pauline Olive…
searchText: which choice completes the text with the most logical transition? in her 2019 essay "cartographies of sound," musicologist iris halberg reflects on a composition that has shaped her thinking: pauline oliveros's 1980 work rose mountain slow runner . the piece, built on long sustained drones and improvised vocal lines, drives the essay's careful inquiry into how listeners experience duration and place. ______ blank in engaging with oliveros's composition, halberg's essay raises questions about the boundaries between musical performance and scholarly writing.

preview: Chilean architect Cecilia Puga has long maintained that contemporary social housing is best understood not as a series of standardized unit…
searchText: which choice completes the text with the most logical transition? chilean architect cecilia puga has long maintained that contemporary social housing is best understood not as a series of standardized units but as a layered, collaborative dialogue between resident, designer, and neighborhood (because residents themselves contribute decisively to a building's shape over time). ______ blank puga organized her recent design studio at the university of santiago, which centered on participatory design workshops with prospective tenants, around the title "housing as conversation."

preview: In 2017, the Smithsonian's National Museum of African American History and Culture mounted a special program, Sankofa/Kibo, featuring the c…
searchText: which choice completes the text with the most logical transition? in 2017, the smithsonian's national museum of african american history and culture mounted a special program, sankofa/kibo, featuring the compositions of memphis-born composer bernadette okuda. as the program's title suggests ( sankofa and kibo mean "returning to retrieve" in twi and "hope" in japanese, respectively), okuda's work bridges several musical lineages. ______ blank her compositions draw substantially on the gospel and blues traditions of the american south and on the techniques of traditional japanese gagaku ensemble music.

preview: When groundwater wells become contaminated by nitrate runoff, the polluted water can be pumped to the surface and processed through an indu…
searchText: which choice completes the text with the most logical transition? when groundwater wells become contaminated by nitrate runoff, the polluted water can be pumped to the surface and processed through an industrial ion-exchange treatment plant. ______ blank the contaminated aquifer can be remediated through bioaugmentation: specialized denitrifying bacteria introduced into the wells consume the nitrates as an energy source and convert them into harmless nitrogen gas, which then escapes into the atmosphere without disturbing the surrounding ecosystem.

preview: The villanelle is a form of fixed-verse poem built around two repeating refrains and a strict pattern of rhymed tercets that culminate in a…
searchText: which choice completes the text with the most logical transition? the villanelle is a form of fixed-verse poem built around two repeating refrains and a strict pattern of rhymed tercets that culminate in a closing quatrain. ______ blank many other poetic forms also depend on rigid meter, recurring lines, and rhyme schemes, but the villanelle is set apart by the obsessive return of its two refrains; the form can feel less like a song than a kind of liturgical chant cycling back on itself.

preview: The fossil Diplomystus dentatus is most often recovered from the Green River Formation in Wyoming. Many of its specimens are preserved in s…
searchText: which choice completes the text with the most logical transition? the fossil diplomystus dentatus is most often recovered from the green river formation in wyoming. many of its specimens are preserved in such fine-grained limestone that even individual scales and fin rays appear sharp and undistorted to the naked eye. specimens of the related fish knightia eocaena from the same deposit are also remarkably well preserved; ______ blank their scales and fin rays usually require a hand lens to be made out clearly.

preview: The "South Sea Bubble"—the explosive rise and equally dramatic collapse of share prices in the South Sea Company in early eighteenth-centur…
searchText: which choice completes the text with the most logical transition? the "south sea bubble"—the explosive rise and equally dramatic collapse of share prices in the south sea company in early eighteenth-century london—is frequently held up as a warning about the dangers of unrestrained financial speculation. yet recent archival work has shown that the scale of public losses and the irrationality of investors have been considerably exaggerated by later commentators. the dramatic narrative typically attached to the episode, ______ blank deserves a measure of skepticism.

preview: Diving Weddell seals—mammals that hunt beneath the Antarctic sea ice—have occasionally been recorded descending past depths of 700 meters.…
searchText: which choice completes the text with the most logical transition? diving weddell seals—mammals that hunt beneath the antarctic sea ice—have occasionally been recorded descending past depths of 700 meters. such extreme depths (most marine mammals seldom dive beyond about 200 meters) clearly mark these record-breaking dives as outliers; ______ blank the great majority of weddell seal foraging dives reach only 100 to 150 meters.

preview: Sociologist Mary Douglas's cross-cultural fieldwork has empirically demonstrated that the rules a community uses to classify foods as "clea…
searchText: which choice completes the text with the most logical transition? sociologist mary douglas's cross-cultural fieldwork has empirically demonstrated that the rules a community uses to classify foods as "clean" or "unclean" reflect deep social distinctions and are not, as earlier theorists had supposed, primarily reactions to hygiene or biological risk. ______ blank douglas's work amounts to a forceful challenge to the long-standing assumption that dietary taboos arise mainly from a community's accumulated medical knowledge.

preview: In aquatic chemistry, a body of fresh water with a pH below about 5 is expected to suppress the growth of soft-bodied invertebrates such as…
searchText: which choice completes the text with the most logical transition? in aquatic chemistry, a body of fresh water with a ph below about 5 is expected to suppress the growth of soft-bodied invertebrates such as freshwater sponges, while a body of fresh water with a ph near neutral typically supports a thriving sponge population. limnologist ji-min park and colleagues, ______ blank documented a dense colony of the sponge spongilla lacustris flourishing in a korean mountain lake with a measured ph of 4.3, far below the threshold at which such organisms are believed to persist.

preview: A whale's fluke—the broad, flat tail used for swimming—looks at a glance like the fin of a large fish. ______ blank the fluke is often mist…
searchText: which choice completes the text with the most logical transition? a whale's fluke—the broad, flat tail used for swimming—looks at a glance like the fin of a large fish. ______ blank the fluke is often mistakenly believed to be vertically oriented and supported by fin rays, as in a fish, when in fact it is horizontally oriented and stiffened by a dense pad of fibrous tissue, reflecting the whale's descent from land-dwelling mammals that propelled themselves with up-and-down movements of the spine.

preview: With its conventional storefronts, parking signs, and overcast sky, Stephen Shore's 1973 photograph Beverly Boulevard and La Brea Avenue, L…
searchText: which choice completes the text with the most logical transition? with its conventional storefronts, parking signs, and overcast sky, stephen shore's 1973 photograph beverly boulevard and la brea avenue, los angeles may strike viewers as offering little reward to anyone hunting for visual depth. ______ blank art historian britt salvesen has built an entire chapter of her book around this single image, arguing that its careful geometry redefines what a vernacular american landscape can be.

preview: Brazilian composer Heitor Villa-Lobos, often celebrated for bending European classical conventions to fit Brazilian folk traditions, did no…
searchText: which choice completes the text with the most logical transition? brazilian composer heitor villa-lobos, often celebrated for bending european classical conventions to fit brazilian folk traditions, did not abandon traditional forms outright; his bachianas brasileiras no. 5 uses a chamber arrangement and adheres to standard movement structure. even in this outwardly conventional piece, ______ blank villa-lobos was charting new territory by binding bach-style counterpoint to the melodic contours of brazilian modinha song.

preview: Many popular histories of the Klondike of the late 1890s emphasize the long, dramatic journey of would-be prospectors from the eastern Unit…
searchText: which choice completes the text with the most logical transition? many popular histories of the klondike of the late 1890s emphasize the long, dramatic journey of would-be prospectors from the eastern united states and europe all the way to dawson city. however, a 2019 study of period steamship manifests and passport records complicates this familiar story; ______ blank researchers concluded, gold seekers who actually reached the goldfields were drawn predominantly from the pacific northwest and western canadian provinces—populations already living within a few hundred miles of the route.

preview: In a long-running debate within paleoanthropology, the field is split between out-of-Africa proponents and multiregionalists. ______ blank…
searchText: which choice completes the text with the most logical transition? in a long-running debate within paleoanthropology, the field is split between out-of-africa proponents and multiregionalists. ______ blank there are those who maintain that anatomically modern humans dispersed from a single african origin and rapidly replaced other hominin populations, and those who hold that modern human traits emerged in parallel from multiple regional ancestor populations through ongoing gene flow. paleoanthropologist rebecca cann places herself in neither camp.

preview: Long considered strictly nocturnal, the desert-dwelling rodent Dipodomys spectabilis , or banner-tailed kangaroo rat, has been observed mak…
searchText: which choice completes the text with the most logical transition? long considered strictly nocturnal, the desert-dwelling rodent dipodomys spectabilis , or banner-tailed kangaroo rat, has been observed making brief excursions above ground during daylight hours—a pattern that biologists had attributed to disturbance by predators or accidental disorientation. ______ blank a recent telemetry study revealed that these daytime trips closely tracked peak flowering periods of certain ephemeral wildflowers and that returning rats carried fresh pollen on their fur, behavior consistent with deliberate, resource-driven foraging.

preview: Solar updraft towers are envisioned as tall hollow chimneys surrounded by a transparent collector that warms air at ground level; the heate…
searchText: which choice completes the text with the most logical transition? solar updraft towers are envisioned as tall hollow chimneys surrounded by a transparent collector that warms air at ground level; the heated air rises through the tower and spins turbines along the way. engineers have long believed that solar updraft towers could one day deliver large-scale baseload electricity to grids in arid regions. ______ blank as of 2024, no commercial-scale solar updraft tower has been completed and connected to a national grid anywhere in the world.

preview: In the early twenty-first century, scholars in literary studies devoted increasing attention to questions of reading practice. Proponents o…
searchText: which choice completes the text with the most logical transition? in the early twenty-first century, scholars in literary studies devoted increasing attention to questions of reading practice. proponents of computational approaches mapped reading patterns across thousands of digitized novels through natural-language processing pipelines; ______ blank literary critic rita felski characterized reading as "a mode of attentive, embodied engagement with a text's affective pull," emphasizing instead the situated, lived experience of individual readers.

preview: Determined to expand access to higher education for Black women in the American South, Mary McLeod Bethune set out to build a school that w…
searchText: which choice completes the text with the most logical transition? determined to expand access to higher education for black women in the american south, mary mcleod bethune set out to build a school that would serve as a lasting institution for her community. ______ blank in 1904, she opened the daytona literary and industrial training school for negro girls with just five students and a few donated supplies, laying the groundwork for what would become bethune-cookman university.

preview: Art Nouveau designers prized organic forms and the appearance of natural growth in their work. ______ blank the wrought-iron Métro entrance…
searchText: which choice completes the text with the most logical transition? art nouveau designers prized organic forms and the appearance of natural growth in their work. ______ blank the wrought-iron métro entrances hector guimard designed for paris in the 1900s weren't actually sprouting from the pavement like vines. still, one detects botanical inspiration in their sinuous curves, tendril-like railings, and lamps shaped like budding flowers.

preview: If the cooling of Earth's atmosphere following the Cretaceous-Paleogene impact had been governed solely by sulfate aerosols ejected by the…
searchText: which choice completes the text with the most logical transition? if the cooling of earth's atmosphere following the cretaceous-paleogene impact had been governed solely by sulfate aerosols ejected by the asteroid, climate models predict that surface temperatures would have recovered within roughly a decade. geochemical records of ocean sediments, however, indicate that depressed temperatures persisted for centuries. ______ blank a second cooling mechanism—likely soot lofted into the stratosphere by global wildfires—must have prolonged the climatic disruption well beyond what aerosols alone could explain.

preview: When composing scores for documentary films, British musician Mica Levi generally records all instrumental parts herself, layering each tra…
searchText: which choice completes the text with the most logical transition? when composing scores for documentary films, british musician mica levi generally records all instrumental parts herself, layering each track in a small home studio. ______ blank for the documentary flipside , which features a cluttered jazz record store, she invited a full ensemble of session players into a professional studio to capture the spontaneous interplay she felt the film required; the resulting score has a looser, more improvisational character than her solo work typically allows.

preview: Paleontologist Maria Reyes and her team have unearthed what they describe as the oldest known winged insect, preserved in a 312-million-yea…
searchText: according to the text, how old is the insect fossil that reyes and her team discovered? paleontologist maria reyes and her team have unearthed what they describe as the oldest known winged insect, preserved in a 312-million-year-old slab of shale in massachusetts. the team has named the new species volusalis paleozoica . they argue that the find pushes the evolution of flight in insects back into the carboniferous period, which lasted from about 299 million to 359 million years ago.

preview: The European Space Agency's JUICE spacecraft recorded a subtle flexing in the icy crust of Europa, one of Jupiter's largest moons. Drawing…
searchText: which choice best states the main idea of the text? the european space agency's juice spacecraft recorded a subtle flexing in the icy crust of europa, one of jupiter's largest moons. drawing on simulations of the moon's tidal stresses and orbital pull, planetary scientist hiro tanaka and colleagues have suggested that this flexing may be caused by warm currents in a saltwater ocean churning below the ice. the team argues that other jovian moons should be surveyed for similar signals of buried seas.

preview: Personal journals kept by composers in the nineteenth century are useful records of musical life, but few are as carefully kept as Clara Sc…
searchText: which choice best states the main idea of the text? personal journals kept by composers in the nineteenth century are useful records of musical life, but few are as carefully kept as clara schumann’s notebooks. beside each entry about her concerts, schumann recorded private reflections on her family and friendships. she also listed the pieces she rehearsed and the audiences she performed for. music historians note that, by spanning more than three decades of changing concert programs and compositional styles, the notebooks are a record of nineteenth-century european concert culture as well as schumann’s life.

preview: For decades, scholars have credited the design of the iconic Park Plaza fountain in Wexford City to architect Lillian Bram, who said she sk…
searchText: based on the text, what piece of knowledge about the park plaza fountain has recently become uncertain? for decades, scholars have credited the design of the iconic park plaza fountain in wexford city to architect lillian bram, who said she sketched the final plan in may of 1908. recently, however, an archivist uncovered a city engineering report dated february 1908 that includes a detailed drawing of the same fountain. the drawing suggests that someone other than bram may have produced the design first.

preview: The following text is adapted from Frances Hodgson Burnett's 1888 novel Sara Crewe . A family has just moved from a noisy city apartment to…
searchText: which choice best states the main idea of the text? the following text is adapted from frances hodgson burnett's 1888 novel sara crewe . a family has just moved from a noisy city apartment to a rambling old farmhouse near a quiet village. the farmhouse was certainly nothing fine to look at; it leaned a little to one side, and mr. halford complained that the floors creaked at every step and that the kitchen chimney drew so poorly it filled the house with smoke. mrs. halford, for her part, was vexed that the windows did not match, and she said the parlor was the gloomiest room she had ever entered. yet the children, who had spent their whole short lives in the close, crowded streets of the city, could not contain their joy. to them, the rambling old house, with its tangled garden and orchard beyond, seemed nothing less than an enchanted castle dropped down into a land of wonder.

preview: In the 1970s, Amadou Diop became a surprise figure in mbalax, a popular form of Senegalese dance music. Most mbalax artists at the time rel…
searchText: according to the text, what is one way that diop differed from other mbalax artists? in the 1970s, amadou diop became a surprise figure in mbalax, a popular form of senegalese dance music. most mbalax artists at the time relied on driving sabar drum patterns and amplified electric guitars to fill outdoor halls. but diop performed alone, accompanying his soft, husky voice with only a small kora. while other mbalax singers tended to write fast, celebratory tunes about contemporary city life, diop preferred to revive older fishermen's songs from his coastal hometown. he also rearranged those songs by stretching their phrases and adding long pauses, which gave the lyrics room to breathe and let listeners feel the weight of each line.

preview: Drumming is a coordinated alarm behavior that researchers have recorded in colonies of leaf-cutter ants. When drumming, sentry ants strike…
searchText: which choice best states the main idea of the text? drumming is a coordinated alarm behavior that researchers have recorded in colonies of leaf-cutter ants. when drumming, sentry ants strike the ground rapidly with their abdomens, producing a sharp vibration that runs through the nest. the signal is set off when army ants begin scouting near a colony, and it warns nestmates to retreat before being raided. researchers suggest that the behavior is a specialized warning aimed at army ants, since the ants do not drum when other, larger predators approach the colony.

preview: Sewing machines were first produced for home use in the mid-nineteenth century across the United States and parts of Europe, dramatically c…
searchText: which choice best states the main idea of the text? sewing machines were first produced for home use in the mid-nineteenth century across the united states and parts of europe, dramatically changing how families could make and mend clothing. with a sewing machine, a household could finish in an hour what had previously taken a full day by hand, and the affordable price of newer models made the technology accessible to working families. people used the time saved to take on side work as seamstresses, sew goods for sale at local markets, and form neighborhood sewing circles that shared patterns and traded fabric.

preview: Archaeologist Tomás Rivera visited the Cerro Blanco rock shelter in northern Peru. While surveying the floor of the shelter, Rivera noticed…
searchText: according to the text, why does rivera think that patolli was played at the site for many generations? archaeologist tomás rivera visited the cerro blanco rock shelter in northern peru. while surveying the floor of the shelter, rivera noticed long rows of small grooves worn into the flat stone. rivera believed the grooves could have been used as a board for early versions of patolli, a strategy game once played widely in the region. some newer grooves had been worn directly over older, fainter ones. this led rivera to think that the game had been played at the site for many generations.

preview: The following text is adapted from Louisa May Alcott's 1869 novel Little Women . Margaret lives with her younger sisters and her widowed mo…
searchText: according to the text, what is true about margaret? the following text is adapted from louisa may alcott's 1869 novel little women . margaret lives with her younger sisters and her widowed mother, mrs. march. margaret, the eldest of the four sisters, had at twenty an unusual measure of self-command and forethought, and even her mother, who herself often acted from impulse, had grown accustomed to leaning upon her elder daughter's quiet sense. she had a warm heart and ardent affections; her sympathies were quickly stirred, yet she had learned to keep them in check when prudence required, a discipline that her mother sometimes resolved to imitate and one of her sisters declared that nothing in the world should ever induce her to practice.

preview: The following text is adapted from L. M. Montgomery's 1908 novel Anne of Green Gables . Anne, a young girl, has just been allowed to keep a…
searchText: which choice best states the main idea of the text? the following text is adapted from l. m. montgomery's 1908 novel anne of green gables . anne, a young girl, has just been allowed to keep a small corner of the orchard as her own plot. anne, who had been used all her life to having very little of her own, found that the small patch of orchard ground was almost more delightful than she could bear. she knelt among the grass and turned the soft earth with her hands, and the longer she worked the more her cheeks brightened. to her mind, there was nothing in the world quite so wonderful as deciding where each new seed should go; she would have gone on planning and planting from morning till night, and counted every hour of it the finest sort of game.

preview: The following text is adapted from a 2021 short story by Renata Cruz. The narrator has just stepped onstage for her first school recital. T…
searchText: according to the text, how does the narrator feel about being on the recital stage? the following text is adapted from a 2021 short story by renata cruz. the narrator has just stepped onstage for her first school recital. the stage lights were brighter than anything i had ever stood under before. my fingers tingled on the keys, and even my breath felt too loud. i was nervous, but i was also proud.

preview: The following text is adapted from a late nineteenth-century novel by an American author. In her small parlor in Boston, an elderly woman s…
searchText: based on the text, how did adelaide pruitt most likely feel when the telegram arrived? the following text is adapted from a late nineteenth-century novel by an american author. in her small parlor in boston, an elderly woman sat absorbed in a worn volume of carlyle's essays. she did not look up when the boy from the telegraph office rapped at the door with a message. she glanced indifferently at the slip of paper he offered and impatiently at the boy himself. adelaide pruitt did not like to be interrupted when she was reading carlyle; and as she was always reading carlyle, an interruption was always treated by her as a sort of impertinence.

preview: The following text is adapted from a 2020 memoir by an author who grew up on a small farm. The narrator is on a long bus ride with her cous…
searchText: according to the text, what did the narrator and diego do during the bus ride? the following text is adapted from a 2020 memoir by an author who grew up on a small farm. the narrator is on a long bus ride with her cousins. diego and ana are her cousins. diego and i traded riddles for hours, trying to stump each other before the bus reached the next stop, and ana liked to count how many hawks she could see circling the fields. we were used to the short rides into salinas and the half-day trip up to stockton, but i had no idea what the overnight ride to los angeles would be like.

preview: The following text is adapted from a nineteenth-century children's novel. Seven-year-old Lina and her elderly cousin Augustina are turning…
searchText: which choice best states the main idea of the text? the following text is adapted from a nineteenth-century children's novel. seven-year-old lina and her elderly cousin augustina are turning the pages of a collection of illustrated nature books. lina had drawn close to the table and was studying with wide and wondering eyes the brilliant plates in the large books augustina was placing before her. all at once she gave a happy cry, for on one page she saw a meadow filled with bright wildflowers, with a small wooden bridge crossing a stream and a flock of swallows wheeling above. with shining eyes lina seemed to drink in every line of the picture.

preview: The following text is from a 2002 novel by a Caribbean American author. The eight-year-old narrator lives with her family in Trinidad. Tía…
searchText: according to the text, why does the narrator pull out the old album of family photographs? the following text is from a 2002 novel by a caribbean american author. the eight-year-old narrator lives with her family in trinidad. tía beatriz is the narrator's aunt. tía beatriz, who was the eldest of my mother's sisters, had moved to canada five years before, when i was only three, and i could not picture her face at all. i climbed onto the chair by the hall cabinet and pulled out the old album of family photographs. but every picture was from long ago, taken when tía beatriz was still a teenager. however hard i tried, i could not put together what she might look like today. my imagination, though, rushed in to fill the empty space.

preview: The following text is adapted from a 1953 novel by an American author. Marcus lives in a row house in South Philadelphia. A soft rain had j…
searchText: which choice best describes what is happening in the text? the following text is adapted from a 1953 novel by an american author. marcus lives in a row house in south philadelphia. a soft rain had just stopped, and the wet bricks of the street were shining under the streetlamps. the block looks well after a rain, marcus thought. from the front step he could see the corner grocer arranging crates of oranges on the sidewalk, two old men playing checkers under the awning of the barbershop, and a small group of girls drawing chalk hopscotch squares on the cooled pavement.

preview: In the 1970s, Yuri Kochiyama led a campaign in New York City to expand education programs for Asian American youth. Her work in this effort…
searchText: according to the text, what did yuri kochiyama lead? in the 1970s, yuri kochiyama led a campaign in new york city to expand education programs for asian american youth. her work in this effort was inspired by her son, billy kochiyama. in 1969, billy joined a series of community meetings organized by the asian americans for action group in lower manhattan. following her son, yuri began attending these meetings too. yuri soon became a member of the steering committee of asian americans for action. she also became the head of what came to be called the chinatown youth education project.

preview: Augusta Savage was a celebrated sculptor who worked in many materials, including her well-known portrait busts, but she was devoted to more…
searchText: which choice best states the main idea of the text? augusta savage was a celebrated sculptor who worked in many materials, including her well-known portrait busts, but she was devoted to more than making art herself. savage also wanted to open the world of art to young people in her adopted neighborhood of harlem, new york. to that end, in 1932 she founded the savage studio of arts and crafts, which offered free art classes to local children, and in 1937 she became the first director of the harlem community art center, a federally funded program that brought visiting artists and instruction to thousands of students.

preview: In the early 1900s, Russian composers experimented with low, dark tones in their orchestral music. They did so by adding instruments that p…
searchText: according to the text, how did alexander glazunov achieve moments of powerful low tones in his symphonies? in the early 1900s, russian composers experimented with low, dark tones in their orchestral music. they did so by adding instruments that played in the deepest registers of the orchestra. for example, in some of his symphonies, russian composer alexander glazunov added contrabassoons and bass tubas to the ensemble. with these instruments sounding together, the orchestra could produce powerful low notes during the most dramatic passages of his symphonies.

preview: In 2017, Daniel Park and his team at a robotics lab set out to design a drone capable of operating beneath the ocean surface. Because seawa…
searchText: according to the text, why would a drone built for the air be unable to operate beneath the ocean surface? in 2017, daniel park and his team at a robotics lab set out to design a drone capable of operating beneath the ocean surface. because seawater is roughly eight hundred times denser than air, the propellers used on standard aerial drones would experience far too much drag to spin properly. for three years, park's team tested designs in tanks that recreated deep-sea pressure and currents. the vehicle the team eventually produced can move through the ocean because its propellers are shorter and turn more slowly than those of an aerial drone of the same size.

preview: Mateo Carvajal cofounded Verde Joven Colombia when he was still in high school. Carvajal and his team founded the organization to teach you…
searchText: according to the text, what is one reason carvajal and others founded verde joven colombia? mateo carvajal cofounded verde joven colombia when he was still in high school. carvajal and his team founded the organization to teach young people how their food choices affect water use and why eating locally grown produce matters. for example, the organization held a community lunch made entirely from produce grown in school gardens.

preview: The following text is adapted from a 2021 novel by a contemporary author. The narrator is a member of her high school's debate team. That s…
searchText: according to the text, what does the narrator do as she walks to the front of the room? the following text is adapted from a 2021 novel by a contemporary author. the narrator is a member of her high school's debate team. that spring, the debate team was invited to compete at the mid-atlantic forensics tournament. walking to the front of the room, i did what my coach had taught me. imagine you're talking to a single friend over coffee. that way you won't tense up. i let my eyes settle on my sister, who sat in the second row, smiling a little, waiting. then i steadied my hands on the podium and began.

preview: Artist Mariana Olvera explained that she wanted to highlight the role of the alebrije, a brightly painted fantastical creature, as a symbol…
searchText: according to the text, which piece of olvera's art was on display in the museum of latin american art in 2019? artist mariana olvera explained that she wanted to highlight the role of the alebrije, a brightly painted fantastical creature, as a symbol in oaxacan culture. to do so, she created many sculptures from layered papier-mâché, a material similar to that used to make traditional alebrijes. in 2019, olvera completed a life-size alebrije sculpture of a winged jaguar, which was displayed at the museum of latin american art in long beach, california. the winged jaguar form is closely associated with the work of master carver manuel jiménez, who is often credited with popularizing alebrijes in oaxaca.

preview: Sound surrounds our daily lives, often shaping how we feel about places we visit and people we meet. This connection has drawn the attentio…
searchText: according to the text, what is one reason some historians are interested in reconstructing sounds from the past? sound surrounds our daily lives, often shaping how we feel about places we visit and people we meet. this connection has drawn the attention of historians who hope to use audio recordings, surviving instruments, and written descriptions of soundscapes to better understand past religious ceremonies, marketplaces, neighborhood routines, and crafts. although background noise can be lost in old recordings and instruments often degrade, recent improvements in acoustic modeling show promise in reconstructing sounds from earlier eras. studies built on these tools may reveal new details about how earlier societies experienced their everyday environments.

preview: To bring her work to more readers, poet Carmen Lozano turned to community broadsides. In broadside printing, a poem is printed on a single…
searchText: according to the text, what is significant about lozano's use of broadside printing? to bring her work to more readers, poet carmen lozano turned to community broadsides. in broadside printing, a poem is printed on a single large sheet, often illustrated and distributed in cafés, libraries, and meeting halls. in her broadside series mothers of the valley (1972–1974), lozano paired short poems about the daily lives of farmworking mothers in the central valley with brief tributes to lesser-known organizers from the same region. the pairing invites the reader to see ties among the women. the broadside format helped lozano's poetry reach a wide audience and supported her aim of building solidarity among latina women through her work.

preview: The average age at which people in the United States buy their first home is 36. Economist Priya Nair studied why younger adults are relati…
searchText: based on the text, what would nair most likely say is a promising way to increase homeownership in young adulthood? the average age at which people in the united states buy their first home is 36. economist priya nair studied why younger adults are relatively less likely to purchase homes and whether there are ways to raise homeownership in young adulthood. nair found that one barrier is a lack of knowledge about how mortgages, down payments, and closing costs actually work; she further found that simply giving young adults clear written guides on these topics noticeably reduces this barrier.

preview: Few animals are known to use tools: among them are sea otters, chimpanzees, and certain crows. But in March 2023 at a marine sanctuary off…
searchText: which choice best states the main topic of the text? few animals are known to use tools: among them are sea otters, chimpanzees, and certain crows. but in march 2023 at a marine sanctuary off the coast of western australia, marine biologist hannah pereira observed a bottlenose dolphin pressing a folded sea sponge over a sharp rock spine to scrape it clean. dolphins had never been seen using a sponge in this way before. behavioral ecologist vincent marlow, who studies bottlenose dolphins, believes the dolphin may have been trying to dislodge a hidden fish or that it may simply have been playing with the sponge.

preview: The following text is adapted from a 1905 novel by an American writer. Edward Foley is being shown, for the first time, a bronze bust that…
searchText: according to the text, what is true about foley? the following text is adapted from a 1905 novel by an american writer. edward foley is being shown, for the first time, a bronze bust that his old friend the sculptor renard has made of him. foley crossed the studio with a careless step and turned at last toward the pedestal. as his eyes fell on the bronze, he gave a slight start and his color deepened with what was unmistakably pleasure. a light of astonishment, almost of recognition, passed over his face, and he remained standing very still, conscious in some dim way that renard was speaking to him, yet wholly unable to follow the meaning of the words. the sense of his own dignity broke upon him as if for the first time; he had never been quite aware of it before.

preview: Acacia senegal , a tree found in the dry savannas of West Africa, is the world's main source of natural gum arabic, a substance used in foo…
searchText: what feature of acacia senegal does the text say is helpful for the process of harvesting gum arabic? acacia senegal , a tree found in the dry savannas of west africa, is the world's main source of natural gum arabic, a substance used in foods, paints, and inks. the tree produces a sticky sap that hardens into the gum. the branches of acacia senegal are helpful for the process of harvesting gum arabic because they have a distinctive feature that lets harvesters collect the sap easily. a pattern of weak points runs along each branch, and when harvesters peel back small strips of bark there, the sap flows out steadily and pools without much waste.

preview: The following text is adapted from a 1910 novel by an English author. Father did not spend all his evenings reading the newspaper by the fi…
searchText: according to the text, what is true about father? the following text is adapted from a 1910 novel by an english author. father did not spend all his evenings reading the newspaper by the fire, or shutting himself away in his study with thick books that the children were never allowed to touch. he was almost always there, ready to teach them little card tricks, mend their toys when they broke, and help them learn the songs they were practicing for school concerts. besides this, he used to invent small puzzles for them while they were away at lessons, and bring them out after supper, and he could be counted on to make up new riddles for every birthday and other family celebration.

preview: In 1936 Holger Cahill was chosen to lead the Federal Art Project (FAP). This project was part of the Works Progress Administration (WPA), a…
searchText: which choice best states the main idea of the text? in 1936 holger cahill was chosen to lead the federal art project (fap). this project was part of the works progress administration (wpa), a program created by president franklin d. roosevelt to provide jobs for unemployed people during the great depression. as the director of the fap, cahill created jobs for over 10,000 painters, sculptors, muralists, and other visual artists across the country. he also helped open free community art centers in cities and small towns, which meant that many people could see and make art for the first time.

preview: When ceramicist Anna Lindgren opened her studio in Portland, Oregon, in 1962, she quickly became known for creating finely thrown stoneware…
searchText: based on the text, what would have been the most likely consequence if lindgren had not begun using a mechanical wheel and a gas-fired kiln? when ceramicist anna lindgren opened her studio in portland, oregon, in 1962, she quickly became known for creating finely thrown stoneware, especially mugs and small bowls. she was perhaps most renowned for her tall vases, which she shaped by hand using a long, careful process. as she gained more customers, lindgren began using a small mechanical wheel and a gas-fired kiln to help her produce vases more efficiently, though she continued to handpaint the floral patterns that made each vase distinctive.

preview: Psychologists wanted to test how young children think about sharing and effort. In an experiment, two adults handed out small treats while…
searchText: which choice best states the main idea of the text? psychologists wanted to test how young children think about sharing and effort. in an experiment, two adults handed out small treats while children (ages five to seven) watched. the adults gave out the same number of treats, but one of them paused to ask each child for help opening the bag before taking a treat out. the children were then asked who was kinder. 68% chose the adult who paused to ask for help. the psychologists think that asking for help showed the children that the adult was treating them as partners. the children may have believed that the adult who did not ask for help did not see them as partners.

preview: Polychlorinated biphenyls, or PCBs, are industrial chemicals that linger for decades in soils, rivers, and forest litter. These compounds a…
searchText: which choice best states the main idea of the text? polychlorinated biphenyls, or pcbs, are industrial chemicals that linger for decades in soils, rivers, and forest litter. these compounds are found in large amounts in wooded floodplains. soil scientist marisol aguilar and her team are studying the role that earthworms play in absorbing pcbs from contaminated floodplain soils. through field experiments, her team has found that these earthworms may be locking up to 4 million kilograms of pcbs each year in their tissues and the casings around their burrows.

preview: In 2023, researchers rediscovered an ancient painted frieze on the walls of a deep sinkhole in southern Mexico. The sinkhole was so narrow…
searchText: according to the text, what challenge did the researchers have to overcome to examine the frieze? in 2023, researchers rediscovered an ancient painted frieze on the walls of a deep sinkhole in southern mexico. the sinkhole was so narrow that there was no spot from which the frieze, which stretched continuously around the entire chamber, could be viewed or photographed all at once. however, the researchers used a remote-controlled drone fitted with a wide-angle camera to capture overlapping images of every section of the wall. they then worked with maya scholars to interpret the procession of human and animal figures painted along the frieze.

preview: Cephalopods—octopuses, cuttlefish, and many other soft-bodied marine animals—have skin that can change color and texture using specialized…
searchText: which choice best states the main idea of the text? cephalopods—octopuses, cuttlefish, and many other soft-bodied marine animals—have skin that can change color and texture using specialized cells called chromatophores. researchers have learned about these skin systems in ancient cephalopods from impressions and chemical traces left in various fossils. for example, in a study of two fossils of the extinct cephalopod species notonautilus glaesarius , lena bachmann, pietro rinaldi, and colleagues observed clear patterns suggestive of pigment cells. they also noted possible traces of muscle fibers that may have controlled those cells. researchers expect to identify more features of the skin systems of prehistoric cephalopods as additional well-preserved fossils are uncovered.

preview: On the high plains of northeastern Bolivia, archaeologist Heiko Prümers and geographer Carla Jaimes Betancourt used airborne laser scanning…
searchText: which choice best states the main idea of the text? on the high plains of northeastern bolivia, archaeologist heiko prümers and geographer carla jaimes betancourt used airborne laser scanning to identify two large pre-hispanic settlements at cotoca and landívar, along with twenty-four smaller fortified sites distributed across the surrounding savanna. the researchers determined that these outlying sites were politically tied to cotoca and landívar because each was built during the same casarabe-culture period and laid out around a central earthen platform with elevated causeways radiating outward, mirroring the larger centers. their matching engineering suggests that the inhabitants of the smaller sites participated in the same agricultural and ceremonial networks that organized life in the two principal settlements.

preview: Paleobotanist Hana Kowalski and her team examined leaf fossils preserved in volcanic ash deposits in Patagonia that are roughly 52 million…
searchText: based on the text, which choice best describes the two previously undocumented broadleaf species? paleobotanist hana kowalski and her team examined leaf fossils preserved in volcanic ash deposits in patagonia that are roughly 52 million years old. the fossils belonged to two previously undocumented species of broadleaf trees from the eocene epoch. the team also noted that the leaves of one of the two species were on average five times broader than those of the largest broadleaf species growing in any temperate forest today.

preview: Geologists studying ancient sea-floor life have catalogued thousands of trilobite specimens from limestone beds in Morocco's Anti-Atlas ran…
searchText: what does the text indicate about the charnwood forest geological formation? geologists studying ancient sea-floor life have catalogued thousands of trilobite specimens from limestone beds in morocco's anti-atlas range, including many dated to the late ordovician period. to investigate the rise of the earliest complex animal communities, however, researchers must look to older sites, such as the avalonian shales at charnwood forest in the united kingdom. protected as a site of special scientific interest, the charnwood outcrops contain more than 1,000 frondlike ediacaran fossils that together capture an early chapter in animal evolution.

preview: An informal contraction of "going to," the verb form "gonna" has long appeared in casual speech across many varieties of English. In recent…
searchText: which question does the text most directly attempt to answer? an informal contraction of "going to," the verb form "gonna" has long appeared in casual speech across many varieties of english. in recent decades, linguists tracking radio broadcasts, podcasts, and news interviews have observed "gonna" appearing more frequently in contexts once reserved for the fuller "going to." what accounts for this shift? researchers note that "gonna" is shorter and faster to say than "going to" and is also better suited to the relaxed, conversational style now favored on much of the airwaves. as broadcasters increasingly adopt that style, "gonna" travels with it into formerly more formal settings.

preview: The European Space Agency's Comet Interceptor mission, led by Geraint Jones, will study a long-period comet entering the inner solar system…
searchText: which choice best states the main idea of the text? the european space agency's comet interceptor mission, led by geraint jones, will study a long-period comet entering the inner solar system for the first time. the mission will focus on the comet's coma—the cloud of gas and dust released as it warms near the sun—which until now has been difficult to sample directly from the comet's pristine outer layers. this pristine coma is thought to preserve materials largely unchanged since the early solar system and to hold clues about how planets formed. using a main spacecraft and two smaller probes positioned around the comet, comet interceptor should help answer long-standing questions about how primitive solar-system bodies behave and evolve when they first approach the sun.

preview: In a study by Hannelore Schmidt, Felipe Aragão, and colleagues, residents of Hamburg, Germany, and Porto, Portugal, were surveyed about the…
searchText: the text makes which point about the difference between the proportions of hamburg residents and porto residents using libraries? in a study by hannelore schmidt, felipe aragão, and colleagues, residents of hamburg, germany, and porto, portugal, were surveyed about their use of public libraries. of the 712 respondents from hamburg, 68.4% reported visiting a public library in the past year, while of the 645 respondents from porto, 79.1% reported doing so. given that the percentage of hamburg respondents who said they lived within a 15-minute walk of a library was actually higher than the percentage reported by porto respondents, simple closeness alone cannot explain the difference in library use between the two cities.

preview: Stretching across more than 900 pages in three volumes, Voices from the Studio Floor , assembled by Priya Banerjee and Lukas Volkov, is off…
searchText: which choice best states the main idea of the text? stretching across more than 900 pages in three volumes, voices from the studio floor , assembled by priya banerjee and lukas volkov, is offered as the "complete, unauthorized oral history" of the long-running animated series starlight atlas as told by the people who made it. the book aspires to be exhaustive by, for instance, including testimony from animators, writers, and voice performers from every season produced to date. but while voices from the studio floor is undeniably a singular and useful reference, it has a limitation common to oral histories: it lacks a unifying authorial perspective that might otherwise weave its many recollections into a coherent overall story.

preview: Using the Atacama Large Millimeter Array (ALMA), a team of astronomers mapped the rotational motion of gas in the protoplanetary disk surro…
searchText: according to the text, what was surprising about the researchers' mapping of the gas in the protoplanetary disk around hd-2089? using the atacama large millimeter array (alma), a team of astronomers mapped the rotational motion of gas in the protoplanetary disk surrounding the young star hd-2089, a dense ring of dust and gas in which planets are still forming. surprisingly, the map revealed a region where the gas spiraled inward in tight, off-center loops rather than tracing the smooth circular orbits researchers had expected. the team had assumed the disk's rotation would resemble the largely uniform, concentric motion seen in protoplanetary disks around other young stars in the same region.

preview: In much of his sculpture, artist Mateo Carrasco prefers suggestive forms over precise representation when evoking specific people or storie…
searchText: which choice best states the text's main idea about carrasco? in much of his sculpture, artist mateo carrasco prefers suggestive forms over precise representation when evoking specific people or stories. in his early work daedalus rising (1971), carrasco renders the inventor daedalus by twisting and stacking aluminum sheets into a tall, winged figure that, while recognizably human, also resembles a half-finished machine. his large copper piece the singing river (2018), which commemorates the poet lucille clifton, takes the form of curved, ribbon-like metal bands that read as flowing water rather than as a likeness of the poet herself.

preview: When threatened, certain species of sea cucumbers expel sticky, thread-like tubes from their bodies that quickly tangle and immobilize atta…
searchText: which choice best states the text's main idea? when threatened, certain species of sea cucumbers expel sticky, thread-like tubes from their bodies that quickly tangle and immobilize attackers. because these tubes are remarkably elastic and adhesive even underwater, materials scientist naledi okafor and her colleagues have been working to reproduce them in the laboratory as a biodegradable alternative to the synthetic adhesives commonly used in medical wound closures. the researchers prefer a lab-made version because harvesting sea cucumbers at the scale that would be needed would be costly and likely damaging to wild populations of the animals.

preview: Houston's Bayou Voices Theater Company, founded by director Naomi Etheridge, stages classic and contemporary plays, from well-known works b…
searchText: which choice best states the main idea of the text? houston's bayou voices theater company, founded by director naomi etheridge, stages classic and contemporary plays, from well-known works by august wilson to recent pieces by playwright aleshea harris. as part of the company's stageshare program, etheridge invites neighborhood residents to attend short workshops on directing and then guide a bayou voices rehearsal of their own, working with the cast for a single scene from one of the season's productions.

preview: Ecologists have predicted that desert lizards' scale structures vary with habitat aridity, but this had not been tested across a single mou…
searchText: which choice best states the main idea of the text? ecologists have predicted that desert lizards' scale structures vary with habitat aridity, but this had not been tested across a single mountain gradient. herpetologist tomás cifuentes examined scales from 187 lizard species living at different elevations—and thus experiencing different humidity levels—on the western slopes of the andes in peru. he found that the scales of lizards from arid, low-elevation habitats not only have a higher proportion of microscopic, water-channeling ridges than do those of lizards from humid, high-elevation habitats, but they also tend to overlap more tightly, reducing water loss across the body's surface.

preview: Since its opening in 2019, the Algae Pavilion—a public exhibition hall in Hamburg, Germany, whose facade is filled with panels of live micr…
searchText: according to the text, why are some critics skeptical of the concept behind the algae pavilion? since its opening in 2019, the algae pavilion—a public exhibition hall in hamburg, germany, whose facade is filled with panels of live microalgae—has become a notable example of bioreactive architecture. engineer hanne vossen designed the building, whose transparent panels circulate algae that absorb sunlight to produce heat and biomass, as a working model for low-emission urban construction. however, the concept has met with skepticism: critics observe that, although the algae strains used in the algae pavilion were carefully selected for the project, it remains too early to determine whether they can sustain their productivity in this unusual setting.

preview: To understand how Neolithic farmers prepared grain for storage, archaeologist Yuna Park and her team tested different drying techniques in…
searchText: which choice best states the main idea of the text? to understand how neolithic farmers prepared grain for storage, archaeologist yuna park and her team tested different drying techniques in a reconstructed pit-house in sweden using replicas of tools recovered from northern european neolithic sites. they tried three neolithic drying methods—open-air racks, shallow hearth pits, and elevated smoke chambers—and concluded that each likely served a distinct purpose. for example, the team found that the shallow hearth pits dried grain much faster than the open-air racks but charred the outer kernels far more often.

preview: The following text is adapted from a short story published in 1899. Margaret is a schoolteacher who keeps a private notebook of poems she h…
searchText: according to the text, why does margaret regularly hide her notebook? the following text is adapted from a short story published in 1899. margaret is a schoolteacher who keeps a private notebook of poems she has written. each evening, after the last of her pupils had gone, margaret would close the schoolhouse door and draw out the small black notebook she kept locked in her desk. at first her landlady, mrs. whittle, had thought it was the natural shyness of a young woman wanting an hour to herself. but as the weeks passed, mrs. whittle noticed something stranger: margaret would push the notebook hastily beneath a stack of arithmetic papers whenever anyone approached, and once, when a visitor from the parish board called unannounced, margaret turned quite pale before she could greet him. it was not the company of others that margaret wished to avoid; it was that any one of them might discover what she wrote.

preview: The following text is adapted from Machado de Assis's 1881 novel The Posthumous Memoirs of Brás Cubas (translated from the Portuguese in 20…
searchText: which choice best states the main idea of the text? the following text is adapted from machado de assis's 1881 novel the posthumous memoirs of brás cubas (translated from the portuguese in 2020 by flora thomson-deveaux). i may well be the most ordinary clerk who ever pushed a quill across a ledger, and, as my landlady used to mutter, the most distracted lodger any boarding house in rio has had to suffer—at least before i came under his tutelage—but i did pass several seasons in the orbit of a singular old astronomer, a man with an unteachable instinct for the heavens. i sat beside his telescope through the cold of many evenings. i watched the patience with which he worked. i saw how a problem would settle into him like a stone into still water, then slowly raise the rings of an answer.

preview: Anishinaabe artist Maeve Jourdain turns hockey sticks used in arenas into sculpture by wrapping them in birchbark and ornaments of Anishina…
searchText: which choice best describes jourdain's approach to art, as presented in the text? anishinaabe artist maeve jourdain turns hockey sticks used in arenas into sculpture by wrapping them in birchbark and ornaments of anishinaabe quillwork. these ornaments include porcupine-quill mosaics and small copper bells, which traditionally adorn ceremonial pouches and ribbon skirts within many anishinaabe communities. jourdain thus combines an object widely associated with competitive sport (a hockey stick) with craft forms historically practiced by anishinaabe women (quillwork and birchbark decoration). in this way, she challenges familiar boundaries between athletic gear and ceremonial craft.

preview: In 1937 physicist Hendrik Casimir predicted that two perfectly flat, uncharged metal plates placed very close together in a vacuum would at…
searchText: which choice best states the main idea of the text? in 1937 physicist hendrik casimir predicted that two perfectly flat, uncharged metal plates placed very close together in a vacuum would attract one another, drawn together by fluctuations of the empty space between them. the so-called casimir attraction remained chiefly a theoretical curiosity, however, until inara petrova and colleagues reported in 2024 that they had directly measured one. the researchers suspended a pair of polished aluminum disks in a chamber and cooled the apparatus, drawing the disks closer until the fluctuating forces between them became measurable. by rotating one disk relative to the other while taking continuous force readings, the team obtained a precise measurement—the first direct quantitative confirmation of the casimir attraction.

preview: The following text is from Yumi Asano's 2020 novel The Slow Conservatory . The narrator and Vélez are both music critics. When my younger b…
searchText: based on the text, what is notable about vélez's behavior? the following text is from yumi asano's 2020 novel the slow conservatory . the narrator and vélez are both music critics. when my younger brother confessed to vélez over tea that he found contemporary classical music, string quartets in particular, somewhat tiresome, vélez pursed his lips, gently lowered his cup, and let out a long, theatrical breath; he then rose and explained, with elaborate apology, that a phone call he had been told to expect had just come to mind, all while making it perfectly clear that no such call had ever been arranged.

preview: The following text is adapted from Rudyard Kipling's 1894 novel The Jungle Book . Bagheera is a black panther who has helped raise Mowgli,…
searchText: which choice best states the main idea of the text? the following text is adapted from rudyard kipling's 1894 novel the jungle book . bagheera is a black panther who has helped raise mowgli, a boy living among wolves. to mowgli, bagheera was all the world. the other folk of the jungle ran together in his eyes like leaves shaken by a single wind. the strangers from beyond the river might call his name as they passed; he heard them as he heard the rustle of grass, and turned his head no farther than the rustle deserved. when baloo, the old brown bear, and kaa, the rock-python, came down through the cane brake on long-promised business, mowgli did not so much as look up till he saw bagheera move forward to meet them; after that he suffered their presence and accepted their counsel as if it were a kind of weather he had agreed to walk through.

preview: The following text is adapted from Anthony Trollope's 1855 novel The Warden . Miss Threadgold was a lady of decided opinions, whose pronoun…
searchText: based on the text, what is true about miss threadgold's neighbours? the following text is adapted from anthony trollope's 1855 novel the warden . miss threadgold was a lady of decided opinions, whose pronouncements upon her neighbours, whether present at her tea-table or absent from it, were marked by a candour at times no less than astonishing. this habit caused her invitations to be received with a measured caution. miss threadgold was perfectly aware of this hesitation among her neighbours, and indeed prided herself upon it.

preview: A common assumption among music historians is that the spread of inexpensive radio receivers in the early twentieth century displaced the p…
searchText: based on the text, what can be concluded about the diminishing popularity of the parlor piano in the early twentieth century? a common assumption among music historians is that the spread of inexpensive radio receivers in the early twentieth century displaced the parlor piano in middle-class households. the declining popularity of the upright piano, which coincided with the rise of home radio, seems to support this claim. however, radio's effect on the parlor piano may be exaggerated. although records of piano shipments to dealers in belgium from 1898 to 1925 show a steady drop in the number of new uprights sold, this downward trend had been established before home radio broadcasting became widely available.
