preview: In the years following the Korean War, some young Korean American women in Los Angeles adopted a striking new look that newspapers of the t…
searchText: according to the text, how do historians of asian american fashion view the kkang style? in the years following the korean war, some young korean american women in los angeles adopted a striking new look that newspapers of the time called the kkang style. they wore boxy, oversized blazers with rolled cuffs, dramatic eyeliner, and short, sharply cut bangs, often paired with motorcycle boots. some commentators criticized the kkang style at the time, saying it was reckless and that the young women ought to dress more conventionally. but historians of asian american fashion see things differently. they view the kkang style as a form of resistance to the era's rigid expectations about gender and respectability. they argue that it expressed the young women's desire for personal expression and autonomy.

preview: Archaeologists have uncovered a clay tablet in southern Iraq that may carry one of the earliest examples of recorded music notation in the…
searchText: which choice best states the main topic of the text? archaeologists have uncovered a clay tablet in southern iraq that may carry one of the earliest examples of recorded music notation in the ancient near east. thermoluminescence testing of nearby pottery indicates that the tablet was likely inscribed between 1500 and 1300 bce. musicologist mariana dorr believes the tablet will be useful in learning more about the use of cuneiform musical signs in late bronze age mesopotamia.

preview: Operators of regional bus systems face obstacles in deciding how many vehicles to deploy on each route: ridership shifts rapidly with the w…
searchText: which choice best states the main idea of the text? operators of regional bus systems face obstacles in deciding how many vehicles to deploy on each route: ridership shifts rapidly with the weather, school calendars, and local events, while ordering and outfitting new buses takes a significant amount of time. researchers lakshmi iyer and diego holub have found that combining operators' own farecard records with data drawn from local mobile-phone usage can dramatically improve the accuracy of such ridership forecasts—by 28 to 49 percent in the cases they directly studied. better predictions mean demand on a given route is easier to meet without operators leaving vehicles idle in the depot.

preview: Tasmania is home to Old Vyne, a stand of more than 18,000 genetically identical Antarctic beech trees that all share a single underground r…
searchText: according to the text, why are ecologists concerned about old vyne? tasmania is home to old vyne, a stand of more than 18,000 genetically identical antarctic beech trees that all share a single underground root network. old vyne is one of the most massive single organisms on earth, but ecologists are concerned that its expansion is slowing in part because invasive feral pigs have been rooting up the young shoots that spring from its base. the ecologists say that sturdy fencing around the perimeter could keep the pigs from reaching the new growth and help old vyne start expanding again.

preview: In 2021, an exceptionally dry winter caused a peat bog in northern Scotland to recede, exposing a wooden tool more than 1,400 years old to…
searchText: what does the text indicate about the discovery of the tool? in 2021, an exceptionally dry winter caused a peat bog in northern scotland to recede, exposing a wooden tool more than 1,400 years old to a hiker collecting birds’ eggs. the tool would typically have rotted within a few decades, but the acidic, oxygen-poor peat had preserved it almost perfectly for centuries. according to archaeologist catriona mackie and her team, the carved handle resembles those used by early pictish craftsmen, although the wood itself shows no signs of fire-hardening, suggesting that any cutting edge must have been made of bone or stone fastened separately to the shaft.

preview: Long a puzzle to planetary scientists, evenly spaced grooves crisscross the surface of Enceladus, Saturn’s sixth-largest moon. A team led b…
searchText: which choice best states the main idea of the text? long a puzzle to planetary scientists, evenly spaced grooves crisscross the surface of enceladus, saturn’s sixth-largest moon. a team led by hanae inami has proposed that these grooves form through a process closely resembling the one that creates parallel crevasses in certain antarctic ice shelves. on those shelves, meltwater pooling beneath the ice freezes and expands, fracturing the overlying surface into regularly spaced channels. although enceladus has no surface meltwater, the moon’s subsurface ocean could provide an equivalent reservoir whose freezing and expansion produces the same striking pattern.

preview: The ancient script used by the Nabataean kingdom of present-day Jordan and northwest Arabia included a distinct numeral for fractional valu…
searchText: according to the text, what do some scholars suggest about the nabataean kingdom? the ancient script used by the nabataean kingdom of present-day jordan and northwest arabia included a distinct numeral for fractional values. the earliest surviving inscription showing this fractional symbol dates to roughly 2,100 years ago. at that time, almost no other writing systems across the mediterranean recorded fractions as a single character. the nabataean use of such a symbol may be even older. some scholars argue that nabataean scribes adapted it from the lihyanite civilization, which thrived in the same region between 2,500 and 3,200 years ago.

preview: The composer Teresa Carreño made significant contributions to the development of nineteenth-century Venezuelan classical music, but her wor…
searchText: which choice best states the main idea of the text? the composer teresa carreño made significant contributions to the development of nineteenth-century venezuelan classical music, but her work is rarely performed in concert halls in europe today except among specialists in latin american composition. one reason for carreño’s relative obscurity in europe is the towering reputation of certain contemporaries. in particular, the composers heitor villa-lobos and manuel de falla have so dominated european programming of latin and iberian repertoire that carreño and other latin american composers of the period are frequently overlooked, despite the technical mastery and originality of their writing.

preview: Domestic dogs can recognize their handler’s emotional state from speech alone and respond more attentively to a distressed voice than to a…
searchText: according to the text, how did the researchers measure how attentively the dogs responded to the recordings? domestic dogs can recognize their handler’s emotional state from speech alone and respond more attentively to a distressed voice than to a calm one. mateusz kowalski and colleagues reached this conclusion by recording the dogs’ tail position and the latency between the playback and the dog’s first orientation toward the speaker while the dogs listened to clips of their handler’s voice played from a hidden loudspeaker. the dogs displayed neutral, slow responses when calm clips were played, but they oriented quickly and held a raised tail when distressed clips were played.

preview: The excavation of a 1,200-year-old Viking burial site in the fjords of present-day Norway has produced a remarkable collection of grave goo…
searchText: which choice best states the main idea of the text? the excavation of a 1,200-year-old viking burial site in the fjords of present-day norway has produced a remarkable collection of grave goods, including hundreds of small glass beads. using a noninvasive laser ablation spectrometer, ingrid halvorsen and her team were able to identify the trace elements within each bead without altering its surface. by matching these elemental fingerprints to those of raw glass cullet still associated with known workshop sites along the mediterranean coast, halvorsen and her team can determine which workshops most likely produced the beads found in the burial.

preview: The following text is adapted from Elizabeth Barrett Browning’s 1850 sonnet sequence Sonnets from the Portuguese , Sonnet 21. Say over agai…
searchText: which choice best states the main idea of the text? the following text is adapted from elizabeth barrett browning’s 1850 sonnet sequence sonnets from the portuguese , sonnet 21. say over again, and yet once over again, that thou dost love me. though the word repeated should seem ‘a cuckoo-song,’ as thou dost treat it, remember, never to the hill or plain, valley and wood, without her cuckoo-strain comes the fresh spring in all her green completed. belovèd, i, amid the darkness greeted by a doubtful spirit-voice, in that doubt’s pain cry, ‘speak once more—thou lovest!’

preview: Historians regard the reign of the Bagrationi dynasty as decisive in the consolidation of medieval Georgia. However, some draw a sharp line…
searchText: according to the text, what is a difference between how historians view adarnase and how they view queen tamar? historians regard the reign of the bagrationi dynasty as decisive in the consolidation of medieval georgia. however, some draw a sharp line between members of the dynasty like queen tamar, whose rule from 1184 to 1213 ce is documented by multiple court chronicles, and less well-attested figures like king adarnase, who is said to have ruled in the 9th century but whose historical existence remains contested. adarnase appears in the life of kartli , a georgian chronicle compiled between 1207 and 1213. however, the chronicle’s account of adarnase relies on legendary material, unlike its records of later monarchs.

preview: Ethnomusicologist Belém Martín-Quintero may be known for her years of fieldwork in Andean radio broadcasting, but her book Currents and Cop…
searchText: which choice best describes the main idea of the text? ethnomusicologist belém martín-quintero may be known for her years of fieldwork in andean radio broadcasting, but her book currents and coplas: listening along the high mountain trade routes is probably her most influential project. the 1988 book, whose title refers to the muleteer trails that connect peru and bolivia, was strikingly unconventional for its moment. it blended memoir, transcribed songs, route maps, and ecological commentary and challenged settled assumptions about what scholarly writing on music could look like. long admired by many practitioners, the book and its author have shaped present-day approaches to writing about regional music.

preview: Among many Pacific Northwest Indigenous nations, totem carvers have traditionally been keepers of clan lineages and records of major commun…
searchText: which choice best states the main idea of the text? among many pacific northwest indigenous nations, totem carvers have traditionally been keepers of clan lineages and records of major communal events. they have often served as historians and ceremonial advisers as well. new tools and materials may have altered some aspects of the craft today, but totem carvers continue to be valued for knowing and protecting the stories of their peoples.

preview: The following text is from a 1902 poem by the American writer Edith Hollowell, titled “The Lamp.” As a small and steady lamp keeps its slen…
searchText: based on the text, in what way is the listening soul like a lamp? the following text is from a 1902 poem by the american writer edith hollowell, titled “the lamp.” as a small and steady lamp keeps its slender flame in the chamber’s quiet air, if the patient hand pour in fresh oil at evening; so doth my listening soul give light, if it be tended with the steady oil of friendship, lacking this, it presently grows wan and falters, even as a lamp left dry upon the table, if the hand of the keeper attend it not.

preview: Recently, climate scientists examined data collected by the European Space Agency’s Aeolus satellite to learn more about the strongest atmo…
searchText: according to the text, what was surprising to scientists studying the data from the aeolus satellite? recently, climate scientists examined data collected by the european space agency’s aeolus satellite to learn more about the strongest atmospheric currents above the south atlantic, sometimes called the polar jet there. the data show that, throughout the season studied, the jet’s core never strayed more than a narrow band of latitude from its midwinter position. this finding surprised the scientists, as they had expected the core to drift broadly between latitudes because of the rapid warming of the surrounding stratosphere. now, scientists believe that long-lived patterns of stratospheric pressure may anchor the jet’s core in place.

preview: The following text is from a 1906 short story by Aurelia Whitmore, “The House at the Crossing,” set in a New England river town referred to…
searchText: what does the text suggest about the crowders? the following text is from a 1906 short story by aurelia whitmore, “the house at the crossing,” set in a new england river town referred to as the town of m. if visitors to the small river town of m. observed aloud that its winters were severe and its diversions few, the townspeople would reply that, on the contrary, m. was a place of much excellent society, that it had a lending library and a singing school, that lectures were given at the meeting-house through january and february, and finally, that quite a number of agreeable households lived there with whom an acquaintance might be cultivated. and they always named the crowders as the most cultivated and most public-spirited household of them all.

preview: The following text is adapted from Pierre Loti’s nineteenth-century novel Iceland Fisherman (translated by Marius Pérard). The narrator is…
searchText: which choice best states the main idea of the text? the following text is adapted from pierre loti’s nineteenth-century novel iceland fisherman (translated by marius pérard). the narrator is aboard a sailing vessel becalmed at midnight off the icelandic coast. the coast has dropped from sight, and we are folded into a hush so deep that the rigging seems to forget its complaints. around us the sea spreads itself out like a sheet of pale metal, and our own sail, struck by the high moon, hangs above us like the wing of some enormous still bird. we no longer call to one another, no longer reckon the watches; we drift along in a profound stillness. the air that holds us up has made of us all creatures resembling itself, mute, content, weightless beings, strangely attentive though without motion.

preview: Convinced that working in an awkward room can sharpen concentration and even improve cognition, architects Henri Vasseur and Luna Ostrowski…
searchText: which choice best states the main idea of the text? convinced that working in an awkward room can sharpen concentration and even improve cognition, architects henri vasseur and luna ostrowski designed an experimental office building in belgium to be more playful than practical. one workstation tilts gently toward a window; a corridor narrows so steeply that two coworkers cannot pass without turning sideways. the effect is disorienting but stimulating: after three years there, software engineer tomasz bergmann reported sustained gains in his focus and mood.

preview: To color her ceramic glazes, Pueblo potter Helena Naranjo-Vigil uses minerals and plants gathered from the canyons of northern New Mexico,…
searchText: which choice best states the main idea of the text? to color her ceramic glazes, pueblo potter helena naranjo-vigil uses minerals and plants gathered from the canyons of northern new mexico, where she lives. for example, she achieved the soft greens and golds visible in her 2017 jar where piñon stands by using rabbitbrush flowers, drying and crushing them before steeping the powder in a wood-ash solution to form a slip. to deepen the appearance of certain hues, naranjo-vigil also occasionally folds in micaceous clay collected from a nearby riverbank.

preview: Discarded tire fragments are tiny pieces of rubber waste. Stretches of urban stormwater channels with higher concentrations of tire fragmen…
searchText: according to the text, what did konaté and colleagues discover about plasticizers? discarded tire fragments are tiny pieces of rubber waste. stretches of urban stormwater channels with higher concentrations of tire fragments also exhibit slower and less turbulent flow. a study by idrissa konaté and colleagues found that the concentration of tire fragments cannot be the only cause of this reduced flow, because the concentration of fragments that would produce the observed effect is much greater than that recorded in these stretches. however, the team found that plasticizers, additives commonly used to soften rubber, leach into the water from the tire fragments and have a much stronger flow-dampening effect.

preview: The following text is adapted from Sarah Orne Jewett’s 1894 sketch “A Lost Lover.” One soft September afternoon, in the course of a walking…
searchText: which choice best states the main idea of the text? the following text is adapted from sarah orne jewett’s 1894 sketch “a lost lover.” one soft september afternoon, in the course of a walking ramble that i had begun without much plan among the back roads of the central counties of maine, i became, with no little vexation, uncertain about the lane i was following. the hills had risen and fallen by such close turns; and the path, for the last half hour, had crossed and recrossed itself between the orchards and the stone walls so confusingly, that i no longer had any clear notion in which direction lay the pleasant village of p——, where i had intended to take my lodging for the night.

preview: When Paraguayan novelist Susana Britos, who had previously published three works in Spanish, began writing in her ancestral language, Guara…
searchText: which choice best states the main idea of the text? when paraguayan novelist susana britos, who had previously published three works in spanish, began writing in her ancestral language, guaraní, in the 1980s, several colleagues and reviewers warned that doing so might place her books out of reach of readers beyond her own region. some pointed out that portuguese — widely read across south america — would be a more practical alternative. rejecting their advice, britos went on to author more than a dozen acclaimed novels in guaraní that have been translated into a total of over twenty languages.

preview: In her 2005 study Patios and Polyphonies , musicologist Lucia Ferreira-Mendes draws her analysis in part on field recordings of street duet…
searchText: what does the text most strongly suggest about the duets sung by beltrão and de sousa? in her 2005 study patios and polyphonies , musicologist lucia ferreira-mendes draws her analysis in part on field recordings of street duets sung in the 1940s in são paulo by the singers dora beltrão and ines de sousa. ferreira-mendes focuses on how beltrão and de sousa improvised the lyrics — replacing the printed verses with pointed commentary on neighborhood politics and sly puns. ferreira-mendes’s work was particularly time-consuming because, in order to transcribe the lyrics as beltrão and de sousa sang them, she had to play the wax-cylinder recordings repeatedly, since the recordings were quite noisy.

preview: Many cultural histories of the Chicano Movement of the 1960s and 1970s rest heavily on manifestos and other explicitly programmatic texts a…
searchText: which choice best describes the main idea of the text? many cultural histories of the chicano movement of the 1960s and 1970s rest heavily on manifestos and other explicitly programmatic texts as primary sources, a tendency that can overrepresent the perspectives of a small number of public organizers, most of them men. historian yolanda estrada-martín has shown that broadening the range of primary sources to include other kinds of material — including mural sketches, family snapshots, and community newspapers — yields a much fuller account of the movement and of the central and varied roles that chicana women played in shaping it.

preview: Although Bohemian-born botanist Thaddäus Haenke completed extensive field journals during his late-1700s travels across South America, his…
searchText: which choice best states the main idea of the text? although bohemian-born botanist thaddäus haenke completed extensive field journals during his late-1700s travels across south america, his notes might have vanished into obscurity after his death in a remote bolivian village. it was czech naturalist tadeáš peregrinus haenke—no relation, but a devoted admirer—who acquired the manuscripts from a missionary in the 1820s, transcribed the deteriorating pages, and supplemented haenke's terse observations with detailed commentary drawing on the latest european botanical literature. the published edition that eventually reached scholars bears as much of peregrinus's editorial hand as it does of haenke's original fieldwork.

preview: The following text is adapted from a nineteenth-century sonnet. The poem is addressed to a beloved person from whom the speaker is separate…
searchText: what is the main idea of the text? the following text is adapted from a nineteenth-century sonnet. the poem is addressed to a beloved person from whom the speaker is separated. the bell hath struck, and night her curtain draws; my weary frame upon its couch i lay, yet sleep, that gentle nurse, withholds her cause, and bids my restless fancy roam astray: for lo! across the leagues that lie between, my spirit goes a quiet pilgrim's round, and lingers where thy distant face is seen.

preview: In 2021, marine biologists documented a dense colony of Pagurus tenebrosus (shadow hermit crabs) clustered around a series of methane seeps…
searchText: which statement about p. tenebrosus and the crab hollow is best supported by the text? in 2021, marine biologists documented a dense colony of pagurus tenebrosus (shadow hermit crabs) clustered around a series of methane seeps on the cascadia continental slope, some 1,400 meters below sea level. sediment temperatures at this site—nicknamed the crab hollow—reach 8°c, considerably warmer than the surrounding 3°c waters. after surveying the site repeatedly over four years, the researchers concluded that the warmer sediments likely accelerate egg development and that the seep field appears to function solely as a breeding ground: thousands of mature adults, freshly emerged hatchlings, and visible egg clusters were recorded at every visit, yet no subadult or juvenile crabs were ever observed in the area.

preview: The following text is adapted from an early twentieth-century short story. Around the harbor of Port Manaca, it is often remarked that Seño…
searchText: based on the text, how do the people of port manaca most likely regard señora quiroga? the following text is adapted from an early twentieth-century short story. around the harbor of port manaca, it is often remarked that señora quiroga carries more household griefs in her market basket than any father confessor carries in his book. the basket, indeed, is conspicuously deep, woven of reed so dense that not a thread of sun escapes it, and lined with a cloth that no fishwife has ever yet contrived to peek beneath. when the señora at last lifts the cloth in the kitchen of her own small house, the sorrows of her neighbors do not come rushing out like startled sparrows; they settle quietly among her potatoes and onions, where they will keep until they are forgotten.

preview: Many music theorists analyze a composition by distinguishing between structure —the underlying harmonic and melodic relations that govern t…
searchText: which choice best states the main idea of the text? many music theorists analyze a composition by distinguishing between structure —the underlying harmonic and melodic relations that govern the piece—and form , the surface ordering of sections that the listener actually hears. in beethoven's symphony no. 5 , the structure rests on the famous four-note motif and its harmonic implications, while the form consists of four movements traditionally labeled allegro, andante, scherzo, and finale. theorist heinrich schenker, however, argued that these two analytic categories cannot fully account for a musical work: in his view, neat technical distinctions overlook the dynamic exchange between composer, performer, and listener through which musical meaning actually emerges.

preview: Until recently, the only fossil evidence of trigonotarbids—an extinct order of small, spider-like terrestrial arachnids that flourished in…
searchText: according to the text, why was mensah and her colleagues' discovery of the australopteron tasmanicum fossil significant? until recently, the only fossil evidence of trigonotarbids—an extinct order of small, spider-like terrestrial arachnids that flourished in the paleozoic era—came from sites scattered across the ancient supercontinent of laurasia. now, in a finding that broadens the known geographic range of the group, paleontologist aisha mensah and her colleagues have described the remains of a new trigonotarbid species, australopteron tasmanicum , that lived roughly 380 million years ago in what was then part of the southern supercontinent of gondwana.

preview: Urban planners around the world are looking for ways to manage stormwater runoff and reduce flooding in increasingly built-up cities, and i…
searchText: which choice best states the main idea of the text? urban planners around the world are looking for ways to manage stormwater runoff and reduce flooding in increasingly built-up cities, and in many cases solutions that draw on natural systems can address these problems effectively. faced with frequent street flooding after heavy rains, the city of copenhagen partnered with a local landscape-design firm to construct "cloudburst boulevards"—parklike streetscapes planted with native vegetation and shaped to channel and absorb storm runoff. the vegetated channels slow incoming water, allow it to soak into the soil, and have measurably reduced flooding on the surrounding streets.

preview: Research on the failure of granular materials has chiefly examined uniform shear flows, in which grains slide steadily past one another. To…
searchText: which choice best states the main idea of the text? research on the failure of granular materials has chiefly examined uniform shear flows, in which grains slide steadily past one another. to investigate a less-studied regime, lena vossberg and colleagues used a custom-built annular shear cell to generate and study collision spikes, brief vertical jets of grains that can erupt when two opposing horizontal flows collide. in ordinary shear flows, the material yields once the applied shear stress exceeds a critical value, capping the peak velocity. vossberg's team found, however, that collision spikes can exceed this velocity cap, because additional factors—such as local packing fraction and the curvature of the colliding fronts—mediate the spike's yielding, not stress alone.

preview: Modernist choreographer Tomás Reyes is credited with the choreography for three full-length ballets: Tideland (1968), Glass Quartet (1974),…
searchText: which choice best states the main idea of the text? modernist choreographer tomás reyes is credited with the choreography for three full-length ballets: tideland (1968), glass quartet (1974), and iron garden (1981). however, the choreography of tideland consisted largely of his rearrangements of earlier sequences by martha graham. in addition, although reyes and collaborator inés calderón devised most of the movement for glass quartet , director marlena voss replaced nearly all of it in rehearsals before the premiere. of the three works, iron garden is the production that contains the most movement actually invented by reyes himself.

preview: The following text is adapted from an early twentieth-century poem. The caged bird beats her wings against the bars, And I, behind my softe…
searchText: based on the text, what challenge do humans sometimes experience? the following text is adapted from an early twentieth-century poem. the caged bird beats her wings against the bars, and i, behind my softer cage of years, beat in like manner at the iron stars that hem my spirit's flight with hopes and fears. her bars are made of brass; my own, of will, and custom that no purpose may unbind; yet hers and mine alike are cages still, and keep the wanderer body, soul, and mind.

preview: The following text is adapted from a late nineteenth-century satirical novel. A crowd has gathered in the courtyard of a country estate bel…
searchText: based on the text, how does the marquis respond to the crowd? the following text is adapted from a late nineteenth-century satirical novel. a crowd has gathered in the courtyard of a country estate belonging to the marquis, an aristocrat who employs a steward called the bailiff. one stout fellow, redder of face than the rest, clambered upon a barrel and bellowed out (as nearly as i could catch it) "down with the bailiff!" the whole company then shouted in answer, though whether it was for or against the bailiff none could plainly say: some were calling out "grain!" and others "rents!" and still others were merely waving their caps. each seemed to roar from a private grievance of his own. all of this i observed from a small upper window of the morning-room, peering over the elbow of the marquis as he watched. "what an extraordinary scene," he repeated mildly to himself. "i have never witnessed such a commotion. and how perfectly of one mind they all are—what splendid harmony of purpose!"

preview: Standard histories of the scientific revolution, such as those by H. Floris Cohen and other Europeanist historians of science, devote littl…
searchText: which statement about aveni and other historians of mesoamerican science is best supported by information in the text? standard histories of the scientific revolution, such as those by h. floris cohen and other europeanist historians of science, devote little attention to pre-columbian mesoamerican astronomy, implicitly framing systematic empirical astronomy as a uniquely european achievement. yet anthony aveni and other historians of mesoamerican science have shown that careful, multi-generational sky observation, predictive eclipse modeling, and other practices characteristic of empirical astronomy were well established in central mexico long before european contact. one reason for the disparity is that europeanist historians of science depend heavily on continuous written records of laboratory protocols and observatory logs, which were largely produced on bark-paper codices that spanish authorities destroyed in the sixteenth century and which therefore survive only in fragments.

preview: In a 2019 paper, Hira Nakamoto and colleagues, conceding that the proposed existence of a fifth fundamental force—an extremely weak, short-…
searchText: which choice best states the main idea of the text? in a 2019 paper, hira nakamoto and colleagues, conceding that the proposed existence of a fifth fundamental force—an extremely weak, short-range interaction supposedly coupling to baryon number—lacked any direct experimental confirmation, reasonably maintained that the hypothesis remained worth pursuing on theoretical grounds because no experiment had yet ruled it out either. a 2024 report by an international consortium, however, presents the first high-precision torsion-balance measurements specifically designed to detect a fifth-force contribution at the predicted range. the measured force profile matched the predictions of the four established forces to within a fraction of a percent, leaving no room for the proposed additional interaction.

preview: The following text is adapted from a twentieth-century novel. The tale of Margit Halász which we now propose to set down—not on her account…
searchText: what does the text most strongly suggest about the tale of margit halász? the following text is adapted from a twentieth-century novel. the tale of margit halász which we now propose to set down—not on her account (for the reader will quickly recognize her as a thoroughly unexceptional, if amiable, young woman), but on account of the tale itself, which appears to us to merit a careful telling (though it must be granted, in margit halász's favor, that it is her tale, and that not every tale falls to the lot of every person)—is a tale that unfolded a great many years ago, has, as it were, the dust of distant decades settled upon it, and consequently must be related in language drawn from the most remote past.

preview: To examine how rising sea-surface temperatures affect zooplankton-mediated carbon export in subtropical waters, Inés Cordeiro and colleague…
searchText: it can most reasonably be inferred from the text that the finding about zooplankton species composition was important for which reason? to examine how rising sea-surface temperatures affect zooplankton-mediated carbon export in subtropical waters, inés cordeiro and colleagues collected intact mesocosms of seawater and resident zooplankton from a 19°c site in the azores and incubated them at a nearby experimental station under conditions matching a 21°c site 200 kilometers to the south. carbon export by zooplankton increased significantly in the warmed mesocosms; importantly, the species composition of the zooplankton community remained the same, which let cordeiro and colleagues attribute the increased carbon export to temperature-driven changes in zooplankton activity rather than to community turnover.

preview: In a recent paper on bulk heterojunction organic solar cells (a class of thin-film photovoltaic device that converts sunlight into electric…
searchText: what does the text most strongly suggest about conventional photoactive layer deposition? in a recent paper on bulk heterojunction organic solar cells (a class of thin-film photovoltaic device that converts sunlight into electricity using blended polymer–fullerene films), priya venkatesan and colleagues describe a method for depositing the cell's photoactive layer using a slot-die coating system. conventional photoactive layer deposition is performed by spin coating a polymer solution onto small substrates, a technique that can waste as much as 90% of the polymer solution and thus drive up production costs at industrial volumes—a constraint that, according to the researchers, slot-die coating, which they characterize as "continuous, scalable, and material-efficient," may largely eliminate.

preview: The following text is adapted from a 1908 poem. The speaker is addressing the painter Henry Ossawa Tanner, an American artist celebrated fo…
searchText: which choice best states the main purpose of the text? the following text is adapted from a 1908 poem. the speaker is addressing the painter henry ossawa tanner, an american artist celebrated for his religious and landscape paintings. thou, with brush of patient skill, hast caught the sigh of prayer, and searched the hush of human will that trembles unaware. the whisper of the leaves, the murmur of the stream, have laid their secrets at thy hand and lingered in thy dream.

preview: Microbial fuel cells are an emerging clean-energy technology that draws on natural biochemical processes. The devices use specialized bacte…
searchText: which choice best states the main idea of the text? microbial fuel cells are an emerging clean-energy technology that draws on natural biochemical processes. the devices use specialized bacteria that consume organic material in wastewater and release electrons as a byproduct; these electrons are captured by an anode and routed through an external circuit to generate electrical current. although the technology has attracted considerable interest from researchers, it cannot yet be deployed economically at the scale required for municipal power generation. to address this gap, scientists in several fields are exploring more durable electrode materials, more efficient bacterial strains, and improved methods for integrating the cells into existing wastewater-treatment infrastructure.

preview: In a Bose–Einstein condensate, large numbers of atoms occupy the same quantum state and behave as a single coherent matter wave that can in…
searchText: based on the text, how are the interactions among magnons believed to be involved in the condensate observed in asante and colleagues' study? in a bose–einstein condensate, large numbers of atoms occupy the same quantum state and behave as a single coherent matter wave that can interfere with itself. until recently, this phenomenon was observable only at temperatures within a few nanokelvin of absolute zero, because thermal motion ordinarily destroys the delicate atomic coherence at any higher temperature. in a recent study, however, naomi asante and colleagues report inducing a bose–einstein-like condensate in a thin layer of magnetic quasiparticles called magnons at temperatures near 290 k. the researchers propose that strong, uniform interactions among the magnons effectively shield the condensate's coherence from the surrounding thermal disturbances of the host material.

preview: Economist Renata Okonkwo has documented a negative correlation between the share of a mid-sized city's labor force employed in tourism and…
searchText: which choice best states the main idea of the text? economist renata okonkwo has documented a negative correlation between the share of a mid-sized city's labor force employed in tourism and the city's ability to attract long-term private capital from outside the region. this finding may seem unexpected—large-scale tourism infrastructure (hotels, transit hubs, convention venues) typically requires upfront capital of the kind that interregional investors are well positioned to provide—but okonkwo observes that the strongly seasonal nature of tourism revenue, combined with the industry's acute sensitivity to broader macroeconomic shocks, tends to produce volatile local economies, generating just the sort of uncertainty that interregional investors generally seek to avoid.

preview: The matorral of central Chile is a species-rich shrubland habitat found nowhere else on Earth. It abuts the Valdivian temperate rainforest…
searchText: based on the text, what role do matorral roots most likely have in maintaining the border between the matorral and the valdivian rainforest? the matorral of central chile is a species-rich shrubland habitat found nowhere else on earth. it abuts the valdivian temperate rainforest along a strikingly abrupt boundary, with virtually no zone of mixed vegetation between the two habitats. matorral shrubs possess unusually deep and broadly spreading root networks that are highly effective at drawing water from the surrounding soil. in a recent experiment, ecologists transplanted valdivian tree seedlings into plots located within the matorral. seedlings placed in plots physically separated from the root networks of matorral shrubs grew nearly four times as fast as seedlings placed in plots in immediate contact with those root networks.

preview: Atomic force microscopes (AFMs) are precision instruments—widely used in materials science and nanotechnology—that drag an extremely sharp…
searchText: based on the text, what is one advantage of coating an atomic force microscope tip with diamond-like carbon? atomic force microscopes (afms) are precision instruments—widely used in materials science and nanotechnology—that drag an extremely sharp silicon tip across a surface to map its features at the atomic scale. the technique has produced remarkable advances over the past three decades, but the contact between tip and sample can subject the surface to significant frictional forces, sometimes deforming or scratching it. to minimize this risk of damage, researchers sometimes coat the tip with a thin layer of diamond-like carbon, so that the wear is absorbed by the coating rather than by the underlying sample.

preview: The following text is from Kazuo Ishiguro’s 2005 novel Never Let Me Go . Bruno is an aging horse kept on the grounds where the narrator wor…
searchText: which choice best states the main idea of the text? the following text is from kazuo ishiguro’s 2005 novel never let me go . bruno is an aging horse kept on the grounds where the narrator works. bruno had grown notably slower over the previous winter, and yet the carers spoke of him as though no time had passed at all. they led him out each morning to the same patch of grass beside the wall, fastened the same blue halter to his head, and stood watching him in the same attitudes they had years ago, when he had been quick and willful. they would scold him gently for his stillness, then laugh, as if expecting any moment that he might bolt off as he once had. it seemed to me that what they were doing was not unkind, exactly, but it was a refusal—a refusal to acknowledge that the creature in front of them was no longer the creature they remembered.

preview: Nineteenth-century philosopher John Stuart Mill is often credited with formulating the “harm principle,” the notion that the only legitimat…
searchText: which choice best states the main idea of the text? nineteenth-century philosopher john stuart mill is often credited with formulating the “harm principle,” the notion that the only legitimate justification for restricting an individual’s liberty is to prevent harm to others. note “credited”: as scholar joel feinberg has argued, mill’s presentation of the principle in on liberty is in fact hedged with so many qualifications and exceptions—regarding children, debtors, and persons unable to govern themselves—that mill cannot reasonably be said to have advanced the absolute, unqualified version of the principle that later commentators frequently attribute to him.

preview: Some astrobiologists searching for signs of extraterrestrial life have proposed that atmospheric CH 3 Cl (methyl chloride) can serve as a b…
searchText: based on the text, ranjan and colleagues would most likely agree with which statement about atmospheric ch 3 cl? some astrobiologists searching for signs of extraterrestrial life have proposed that atmospheric ch 3 cl (methyl chloride) can serve as a biosignature gas—a marker indicating that a planet may host life. sukrit ranjan and colleagues examined this possibility, finding that on rocky planets orbiting cool dwarf stars, ch 3 cl produced by oceanic microbes could in principle accumulate to detectable concentrations because such stars emit relatively little ultraviolet radiation, and ultraviolet light is what ordinarily destroys ch 3 cl in planetary atmospheres. around hotter stars, by contrast, the gas would break down too quickly to be a reliable indicator.

preview: For most of the twentieth century, the prevailing view of the hippocampus—a small, seahorse-shaped structure in the vertebrate brain—center…
searchText: which choice best states the main idea of the text? for most of the twentieth century, the prevailing view of the hippocampus—a small, seahorse-shaped structure in the vertebrate brain—centered almost exclusively on its role in forming and consolidating new memories. this focus on memory, while well supported, has obscured other roles the structure plays in cognition. research over the past two decades has steadily accumulated evidence that the hippocampus is also engaged when individuals imagine future events, make spatial decisions, and even reason about abstract relationships among objects. as a result, neuroscientists increasingly describe the hippocampus not as a dedicated memory center but as a region whose contributions extend across a range of cognitive functions that share certain underlying computational demands.

preview: Paleontologist Mei Wang and colleagues have reported the discovery, at a quarry in northeastern China, of dozens of fossilized burrows from…
searchText: what does the text most strongly suggest about the site discovered by the researchers? paleontologist mei wang and colleagues have reported the discovery, at a quarry in northeastern china, of dozens of fossilized burrows from the early cretaceous period, along with the remains of small mammal-like creatures preserved inside several of them. the researchers conclude that the area was once a long-occupied denning ground used by these animals across multiple generations. they base this conclusion in part on the density of burrows at the site, the variety of ages of the individuals preserved, and the gradual layering of sediment within the burrows, which the team argues is consistent with repeated reuse over an extended interval rather than a single catastrophic event.

preview: The following text is from Henrik Ibsen’s 1879 play A Doll’s House . Nora has lived with her husband, Torvald, for several years and has ju…
searchText: in the text, which point does nora most directly make about herself and her opinions? the following text is from henrik ibsen’s 1879 play a doll’s house . nora has lived with her husband, torvald, for several years and has just begun to question whether she truly knows herself outside of the role he has assigned her. nora: it is true, torvald. when i lived at home with father, he told me what he thought of everything, and so i had the same opinions; and if i differed from him i concealed the fact, because he would not have liked it. he called me his doll-child, and he played with me as i played with my dolls. and when i came to live with you— torvald: what sort of expression is that to use about our marriage? nora: i mean that i was simply transferred from father’s hands into yours. you arranged everything according to your own taste, and so i got the same tastes as yours—or else i pretended to, i am really not quite sure which.

preview: Some studies of plant growth involve raising plants outdoors in real soil under variable weather, and some involve raising plants in contro…
searchText: which choice best states the main idea of the text? some studies of plant growth involve raising plants outdoors in real soil under variable weather, and some involve raising plants in controlled greenhouses with carefully regulated light, temperature, and nutrients. each approach has advantages. in outdoor studies, researchers can be confident that the plants are responding to genuinely natural conditions, but they also have less ability to identify which environmental factor caused any given outcome. in greenhouse studies, researchers can isolate a single variable cleanly, but the conditions are so unlike a real growing environment that the results may not transfer reliably to the field. selecting the right approach therefore depends on what kinds of questions a particular study is designed to answer.

preview: Poetry in Classical Tamil, the language of the early Sangam Age in southern India, relies on the convention of tinai , in which an outwardl…
searchText: which statement about the kurinji tinai is most strongly supported by the text? poetry in classical tamil, the language of the early sangam age in southern india, relies on the convention of tinai , in which an outwardly literal scene—a specific landscape with its associated flora, animals, and human activities—is understood by readers to evoke a particular interior emotional state. for example, the kurinji tinai, named after a mountain flower that blooms only rarely, describes mountainous terrain at night but is understood to depict the union of lovers. readers familiar with the convention recognize that the kurinji setting is not the central subject of the poem; the mountain landscape functions as a vehicle for representing a state of heightened romantic feeling.

preview: The following text is from Sara Teasdale’s 1920 poem “Winter Dusk.” When the long day at last has drawn its breath And every bird is silent…
searchText: which choice best states the main idea of the text? the following text is from sara teasdale’s 1920 poem “winter dusk.” when the long day at last has drawn its breath and every bird is silent in the boughs, and shutters tighten on the windowed house, and the slow shadow climbs the eastern path to stand a moment by the orchard wall— then i, who have walked far and seen the snow lay its white hand upon the fields below, come home to find the kettle, and the small bright window of the kitchen, and the chair pulled close, and the warm patient room aware.

preview: Modern wheat varieties are largely the product of more than a century of breeders crossing certain plants to select for particular agricult…
searchText: which choice best states the main idea of the text? modern wheat varieties are largely the product of more than a century of breeders crossing certain plants to select for particular agricultural traits, such as high yield, short stature, and resistance to specific diseases. farmers often say that some varieties are also more likely than others to express particular non-target traits—some are said to handle drought better; others, to produce flour with a sweeter taste. yet recent work by plant geneticists studying field trials of dozens of widely planted varieties has found that the variation in such traits across varieties is, on average, smaller than the variation observed among individual plants of the same variety grown under different soil and weather conditions. in other words, where and how a variety is grown appears to influence these traits more than the variety itself does.

preview: Lichen-forming fungi rely on a symbiotic partnership with algae or cyanobacteria living within their tissues, the photosynthetic partner pr…
searchText: what does the text most strongly suggest about lichens that display striking yellow or orange hues? lichen-forming fungi rely on a symbiotic partnership with algae or cyanobacteria living within their tissues, the photosynthetic partner producing sugars that the fungus cannot make for itself. when this partnership is healthy, many lichens appear in muted greens, browns, or grays, reflecting the pigments of their internal photosynthetic colonies. in response to severe environmental stressors such as prolonged drought or extreme heat, however, lichens sometimes expel a portion of their photosynthetic partners. with fewer of those colonies remaining in their tissues, certain other pigments produced by the fungus itself, which were previously masked, become visible, and the lichen may take on striking yellow or orange hues until the population of partners recovers.

preview: Reggaeton remains one of the most dismissed popular music genres of the past three decades. But as scholars increasingly argue, the genre r…
searchText: what does the text most strongly suggest about the reggaeton genre? reggaeton remains one of the most dismissed popular music genres of the past three decades. but as scholars increasingly argue, the genre rewards much closer attention than many critics have been willing to give it. take the case of reggaeton pioneer ivy queen: her early hits frequently engaged with themes of gender autonomy, consent, and economic precarity in ways that drew on rhetorical traditions from puerto rican feminist activism. treating her songs as merely danceable party tracks, scholars contend, misses how carefully she constructed her lyrics—and underestimates the social and political work the genre has been doing throughout its development.

preview: In a study of online education, Priya Subramaniam et al. examined moderate frustration (the mild irritation that occurs when a task is hard…
searchText: which choice best states the main idea of the text? in a study of online education, priya subramaniam et al. examined moderate frustration (the mild irritation that occurs when a task is harder than a learner expects) to determine whether it could yield positive learning outcomes. the team focused on adult students who had previously taken several online courses and who reported that a particular new course was harder than they had anticipated. counterintuitively, the researchers found that the students who reported moderate frustration during the course generally completed it at higher rates and scored higher on a follow-up retention test than peers who reported either no frustration or extreme frustration. subramaniam’s team suggests that moderate frustration may prompt learners to slow down, revisit material, and develop more durable understanding.

preview: The following text is adapted from an 1820s epistolary novel. The text is an excerpt from a letter by Lady Cresswell to her cousin, Mr. Pen…
searchText: based on the text, lady cresswell would most likely agree with which statement about mr. pendry? the following text is adapted from an 1820s epistolary novel. the text is an excerpt from a letter by lady cresswell to her cousin, mr. pendry, the tutor of her young niece, cecily, regarding an invitation to accompany lady cresswell’s family on a season in bath. it is most earnestly our wish to enliven our small party by the addition of dear cecily, who would share, equally with my own daughters, the affection and care of our household. do not protest before you have weighed the matter; it is time that the child be permitted to see something beyond the parsonage. when the young are kept too closely from the world, their lively fancy paints it to them as a place of wonders from which they have been wrongfully shut out; but when they are shown it gently, and in proper season, they come to see it as it is, a place at once of consolation and of trial, of hope as much as of disappointment.

preview: In 2022, 12 previously unknown asteroids were confirmed to share an orbital region near Jupiter. Nine of the asteroids travel in the same d…
searchText: based on the text, which choice best describes the asteroid with the elongated orbit? in 2022, 12 previously unknown asteroids were confirmed to share an orbital region near jupiter. nine of the asteroids travel in the same direction as the bulk of nearby objects, while the other three travel in the opposite direction. all but one of the 12 asteroids follow nearly circular orbits at roughly the same distance from the sun. the remaining asteroid, by contrast, follows a highly elongated orbit—sometimes drifting much closer to the sun and sometimes much farther away than its companions—and travels in the same direction as the majority of the group.

preview: The following text is from Hilary Mantel’s 2009 novel Wolf Hall . The narrator is describing Stephen Gardiner, a senior advisor at the cour…
searchText: based on the text, which choice best describes stephen gardiner? the following text is from hilary mantel’s 2009 novel wolf hall . the narrator is describing stephen gardiner, a senior advisor at the court of king henry viii. gardiner came into the council chamber with the brisk, weary expression of a man who has already won an argument that no one else has yet been told they are having. he took his seat without greeting and laid out his papers as though placing pieces on a board, slow and certain. when he finally looked up, it was with the practiced patience of someone who had decided in advance to be unsurprised by whatever the others might say. even before he spoke, the room seemed to have been arranged to suit him.

preview: Philosophers note that many people share an intuitive sense that while breaking promises is generally wrong, there are circumstances in whi…
searchText: which choice best states the main idea of the text? philosophers note that many people share an intuitive sense that while breaking promises is generally wrong, there are circumstances in which doing so seems clearly permissible. if this intuition is correct and we lack an absolute duty to keep our promises, what underlies our general opposition to breaking them in the first place? mira halpern offers a response: she argues that promises function less as inviolable rules and more as commitments that generate strong but defeasible expectations in others. on her view, breaking a promise is wrong primarily because—and to the extent that—it betrays the reasonable expectations the promise was understood to create. when other moral considerations override those expectations, breaking the promise can be justified without contradicting the original commitment’s ordinary force.

preview: The most recent staging of the participatory performance piece Threshold Hours , which opened in Berlin in 2019, converts its performance s…
searchText: what does the text most strongly suggest about threshold hours ’s use of its performance space? the most recent staging of the participatory performance piece threshold hours , which opened in berlin in 2019, converts its performance space—an abandoned subway station—into a stylized depiction of a 1970s-era city. audience members, who roam the platforms and tunnels at their own pace, encounter performers playing commuters, ticket clerks, and street vendors in scenes that unfold in multiple locations simultaneously. significantly, the design team treats the station’s peeling tiles, exposed wiring, and water-stained walls not as defects to be masked but as defining textures of the production: the same patch of corridor can serve, depending on the angle and lighting in a given scene, as a bustling thoroughfare, a hidden alley, or the dimly lit edge of a half-remembered dream.

preview: Several scholars have argued that conditions in northern Italy during the mid-thirteenth through early fourteenth centuries—namely, expandi…
searchText: which choice best states the main idea of the text? several scholars have argued that conditions in northern italy during the mid-thirteenth through early fourteenth centuries—namely, expanding urban literacy alongside frequent political clashes between rival city-states—were uniquely conducive to the flowering of early italian vernacular literature. stefania lombardi’s recent analysis of regional record-keeping practices, however, complicates this picture. lombardi argues that the rise of vernacular writing in the period was at least as much a product of changes in commercial bookkeeping conventions, which gave merchants and notaries practical reasons to record contracts and correspondence in spoken italian rather than latin. on her account, attending narrowly to literacy and political conflict obscures the central role that the everyday demands of commerce played in establishing the vernacular as a written medium.

searchText: which statement, if true, would most directly support tagawa’s claim? the japanese aesthetic concept of wabi-sabi —an appreciation for impermanence, imperfection, and the patina of age—has long shaped traditional crafts such as tea-ware and architecture. scholar naomi tagawa cautions that translating wabi-sabi simply as “rustic beauty” in english-language texts is misleading: it reduces the concept to a purely visual style and obscures the ethical and contemplative dimensions of wabi-sabi as a philosophy of how to live with material things over time.

preview: In a series of experiments, researchers Olivia Renner and Marcus Boyd showed that the background music played in a restaurant can influence…
searchText: which finding from the cafeteria-and-fine-dining experiment, if true, would most directly support the conclusion presented in the text? in a series of experiments, researchers olivia renner and marcus boyd showed that the background music played in a restaurant can influence diners’ ratings of the food. participants in several experiments rated the same dishes in identical (generic) dining-room settings as significantly more enjoyable when slow classical music was playing than when no music played. in one experiment, however, renner and boyd served the same meal with slow classical music in two settings: a fine-dining establishment and a casual cafeteria. the result of this experiment suggests that a casual-dining context may moderate the effect renner and boyd observed in their other experiments.

preview: Since the 1990s, music critics and broadcasters in Western Europe have grouped a wide variety of musical traditions from East Asia under th…
searchText: which finding about pansori and mongolian throat-singing, if true, would most directly support kim’s claim? since the 1990s, music critics and broadcasters in western europe have grouped a wide variety of musical traditions from east asia under the umbrella term “asian fusion.” while some welcome this label for raising the visibility of traditions like korean pansori and mongolian throat-singing in european concert programs, musicologist hyun-joo kim argues that the term flattens highly distinct musical lineages by treating them as variants of a single style.

preview: Paleobotanist Vera Lindstrom and colleagues recently identified a set of fossilized leaves at a Carboniferous-period site in Scotland that…
searchText: which statement, if true, would most directly weaken lindstrom and colleagues’ claim about the fossils found in germany and morocco? paleobotanist vera lindstrom and colleagues recently identified a set of fossilized leaves at a carboniferous-period site in scotland that they claim are the oldest known examples of a particular kind of fern. over the past decade, two sites in germany and morocco have produced leaf fossils of comparable age that several other researchers have argued are most likely ferns of the same kind, based on the leaves’ vein patterns and outline shapes. but lindstrom and colleagues argue that what appear to be diagnostic veins on the german and moroccan fossils are in fact compression artifacts from sedimentation, and they conclude that those fossils belong to an unrelated plant lineage.

preview: Many veteran translators will tell you that judging the quality of a literary translation depends not only on accuracy and fluency but also…
searchText: which finding, if true, would most directly support the researchers’ contention? many veteran translators will tell you that judging the quality of a literary translation depends not only on accuracy and fluency but also on intuitions about voice and register developed over years of practice. in a recent study, however, researchers trained a large language model on thousands of paired source texts and professional translations and found that the model could rank candidate translations roughly as well as a panel of expert translators. some translators have voiced concern that they might be replaced by such models, but the researchers contend that this outcome is highly unlikely.

searchText: which finding from the radar survey, if true, would most directly support vega’s conclusion? archaeologists had long held that the norte chico civilization of the central peruvian coast, which flourished in the third millennium bce, was characterized by small fishing villages with little large-scale construction. recently, however, a team led by marisol vega used drone-mounted ground-penetrating radar to map the buried foundations beneath several coastal sites, and the team concluded that the norte chico people in fact developed a form of monumental, planned urbanism along that coast.

searchText: which finding, if true, would most directly support saito, quintero, and erikson’s claim? plesiosaurs were long-necked marine reptiles that lived during the jurassic period, roughly 200 million to 145 million years ago. researchers hana saito, pablo quintero, and lila erikson examined oxygen-18 isotopes in plesiosaur bone tissue to estimate likely body temperatures, and they concluded that plesiosaurs were endothermic—that is, they used internal metabolic processes to maintain a stable body temperature across varying ambient temperatures. saito, quintero, and erikson claim that endothermy would have enabled plesiosaurs to range into relatively cold subpolar waters.

preview: Engraved bronze mirrors with figural decoration were a hallmark of ritual life in many Iron Age communities in Western Europe. One of the e…
searchText: which finding, if true, would most strongly support the underlined explanation? engraved bronze mirrors with figural decoration were a hallmark of ritual life in many iron age communities in western europe. one of the earliest known examples, the aberlawr mirror, was unearthed in southern wales in 1972. its overall geometric framing resembles that of other celtic metalwork from the la tène period (450 bce–50 ce). the central motif, however, is unmistakably a lion—a species that did not live in britain at the time. lions appear frequently in ancient etruscan art, and the etruscan heartland in central italy fell well within the species' historical range. on these grounds, some scholars argue that long-distance trade in etruscan luxury objects carried the imagery as far as wales, where a celtic artist could have adopted the lion motif and worked it into the aberlawr mirror.

searchText: which finding, if true, would most directly support the team's conclusion? in vertical inheritance, organisms pass genes to their offspring; in horizontal transfer (ht), genetic material moves between unrelated species, often via microbes. in a 2024 study, ichthyologist mariana costa and her team investigated possible ht events between large freshwater predators and small prey fishes in the amazon basin. the team detected htl-1 —a transposable element transmitted vertically in piranhas—in many tetra species that piranhas regularly consume. the apparent direction of transfer is puzzling: tetras rarely survive an encounter with a piranha and so would seem unable to pass an acquired element to their offspring. nonetheless, the team concluded that htl-1 is genuinely transmitted from piranhas to tetras, either directly or indirectly, through ht.

searchText: which finding, if true, would most directly support the idea advanced by rao and mendoza? as workplace learning has become increasingly app-based and notification-driven, deliberate professional development has given way to an attitude known as "updates find me" (ufm), in which employees passively rely on push alerts and colleagues' posts to keep up with developments in their field. educational psychologists aditi rao and carlos mendoza analyzed survey data from a representative sample of mid-career engineers to measure strength of ufm attitude, technical knowledge, and engagement with their professional community. although no major professional credentialing exam took place close enough to the study to allow rao and mendoza to establish a causal link between ufm and credential renewal, they nevertheless posited that ufm may lower the probability of credential renewal through an indirect effect.

searchText: which finding, if true, would most directly support the botanists' hypothesis? on the basalt cliffs of iceland's southern coast, saxifraga rivularis and saxifraga oppositifolia —two plants in the saxifragaceae family—establish themselves on bare, nutrient-poor surfaces of basalt rock. botanists sigríður jónsson and lukas berger used micro-ct imaging to show that the roots of s. rivularis and s. oppositifolia , which grow directly into the basalt, develop dense clusters of fine hairs near the root tips; further chemical analysis revealed that these hairs secrete both oxalic and tartaric acids. the botanists hypothesize that the plants depend on dissolving the underlying basalt with these acids, as the process not only opens channels for continued root growth but also releases the iron oxides that supply the vital nutrient iron.

searchText: which statement, if true, would most strongly support the claim made by liu and colleagues about the cause of borisov-ii's acceleration? first observed in 2023, the interstellar visitor known as borisov-ii became a source of puzzlement when astronomers noticed that its acceleration could not be fully accounted for by the gravitational pull of nearby planets and the sun: some additional, nongravitational influence had to be acting on its velocity and trajectory. earlier proposals invoked unusual scenarios—for instance, that borisov-ii might be partly composed of two loosely bound fragments whose geometry distorts its motion—but these have been judged unrealistic. astrochemist liu wei and colleagues now propose that the nongravitational acceleration is produced by the venting of trapped carbon dioxide gas from borisov-ii's ice-rich nucleus.

searchText: which finding from diop and salazar’s research, if true, would most directly support their claim? sorghum ( sorghum bicolor ) is a drought-tolerant grain that is widely consumed in west africa, but it is somewhat difficult to digest because of its high content of resistant starch and condensed tannins. sorghum grains also contain phytate, an antinutrient that binds to dietary minerals such as iron and zinc and reduces the body’s ability to absorb them. in a research article, food scientists aminata diop and beatriz salazar describe a controlled malting process—sprouting the grains under specified humidity and temperature—that they argue makes sorghum simultaneously more digestible and more nutritious than conventionally prepared sorghum. to validate this claim, however, the malting process would need to be shown to lower the levels of compounds that hinder digestion or mineral absorption without merely degrading other valuable components of the grain.

preview: Glacier-fed lakes typically appear turquoise or milky white. The milky-white tint comes from very fine rock particles, called “glacial flou…
searchText: which finding, if true, would most directly weaken the team’s hypothesis? glacier-fed lakes typically appear turquoise or milky white. the milky-white tint comes from very fine rock particles, called “glacial flour,” which the glacier grinds from bedrock and which scatter incoming sunlight. when the particles are slightly larger or sparser, the lake tends to look turquoise because longer wavelengths are absorbed and shorter (blue-green) wavelengths are preferentially scattered. however, several glacier-fed lakes in the karakoram range have recently been observed to look bright emerald green. one team of glaciologists has hypothesized that this unusual color is due to traces of dissolved iron-rich organic acids that wash into the lakes from surrounding soils each spring; the team proposes that these acids impart a faint yellow tint that, mixed with the lake’s underlying blue, produces the observed green.

searchText: which finding, if true, would most directly undermine mbatha’s hypothesis? when grazing along estuarine mudflats, fiddler crabs damage the rhizomes of cordgrass plants growing in the sediment. along several stretches of coastal georgia, the fiddler-crab population is dense and well established, yet the cordgrass meadows there are healthier than those found elsewhere along the southeastern coast. to explain this, marine ecologist adaeze mbatha and colleagues compared the georgia meadows with meadows at sites where fiddler crabs are absent or only recently established and concluded that long-term damage from fiddler crabs improves cordgrass meadow health by promoting clonal regrowth of rhizome tissue.

searchText: which finding from the study, if true, would most directly support the researchers’ conclusion? most works of long-form fiction make implicit choices about how characters travel between places, including the modes of transport that authors take for granted as available to ordinary characters. in a 2024 study, researchers hye-jin park and marco conti argue that an observable pattern in such references reflects a shift in everyday human mobility prompted by the mass adoption of bicycles in late-nineteenth-century europe. the researchers drew this conclusion by analyzing a large digital corpus of european novels published between 1820 and 1920, examining how frequently characters were described as walking, riding in horse-drawn vehicles, or using bicycles to traverse short urban distances.
